### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T18:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-35300>

If anyone is still reading these posts for more verification that they should do this all I have to say is YOU MUST DO THIS!!! I sent over so many lab, but only the last nine years worth and he plowed through all of them and tied a lot together for me. I took notes and recorded it and like the blogs and webinars, I will need to go over this several times to better understand. It seemed to go so fast! I have a lot going on in my body but it's not all bad. He gave me insight into things to work on now and things that may be problematic later on. I cannot say enough good things about my experience. Plus to hear that I have good genes (thanks Mom) made all the difference in the world.

Thank you Dr. Kruse. Although it wasn't all good news, the good news was fantastic! I appreciate all your knowledge and the time you took to share it with me. I feel like this is a turning point in my life and now I need to take the next right action. What a great Fourth of July present I was given!

### Lanie — 2012-07-04T06:31:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-35433>

I had my first consultation today with Dr. K. and it was more than eye-opening. As others have said, he zeroed right in on what was holding back my progress. It was obvious to him after reading my family history and my labs. He was even able to tell me what my 2-year old daughter is likely to need to avoid the existing multi-generational family pattern of hormonal mayhem. I'm a little overwhelmed by all the information transmitted in that half-hour. I was nervous before-hand, but Dr. K. was very easy to talk to. I left the call with an plan of action for prioritizing and addressing the various issues that were addressed. I would recommend a consult to anyone on this journey, but especially if you're stuck. The cost is minimal compared to the benefit.

### Lanie — 2012-07-04T06:33:25+0300

### Tizawee — 2012-07-04T20:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-35505>

This morning’s consult was awesome. Dr. K was patient, funny and, when needed, firm with me. He was able to point out, just from labs, some places in which I’ve been lax with myself and others where I’m doing quite well. My three biggest areas to work on are: less light at night, hormones, and inflammation. He steered me on a clear, simple path to get these issues handled. It’ll take some time but now I have confidence that I have the knowledge and tools to get to Optimal. So, I’ve got blood tests and supplements on order. Right now I’m off to Home Depot for some 25 watt bulbs.

If there’s any question at all in your mind as to whether you should do a consult with Dr. Kruse or not, my advice is to do as much of a consult as you can afford as soon as you can afford it. It goes a long way toward hope that this broken body can be fixed so life will be more normal, and toward where to focus your efforts rather than shooting in the dark.

I am a Starfish on a Mission from Kruse!

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-04T21:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-35509>

Dr, Jack,

You shook me to my core and i am sooo grateful. I have been reading the blog and later the forum for about 3 months. I was pretty sure I knew where my problems were...cortisol, thyroid. I was blown away when he came up with what what my P/E2 ratio really meant and that was my #1 problem. I am still shaking a bit can't seem to settle my system.

What I'm saying here is find out from the Pro..he is a gem and the consult fee is nothing in the big picture. You may be focusing in the wrong place as I was. Find the money. I can say this because my hub has only worked 4 months in the last 17(he's 62) and my job was just to keep me busy, so I don't make alot of money, but I have good health insurance and I feel like I'm helping people everyday even if it's just a big smile or a pat on the back...I'm working on how I can pay for the added tests I need-no question I will find a way--sometimes your life really does depend it.

Don't hesitate, I am shook up, but I'm thrilled about the future and reaching Optimal. I can see the light and I've never been afraid of work!

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-04T21:13:08+0300

Gretchen...thanks for the recording links..I wish i had seen them before my consult yesterday.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-09T15:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36313>

I just wanted to post that it has been one week since the educational consult and the webinar. Based on our conversation, reviewing the labs, and going deeper into the webinar I am in a big happy change. After that day I changed my supplements based on my hormone panel. I woke up this morning and noticed that in one week the skin on the back of my hands has probably dropped 20 years in appearance and in age. The skin tugor has returned, the patchiness is gone, the softness is there again. This is without putting lotion on them. My hands will be 55 years old next month. Woohoo! Thanks, Dr. K.!

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-09T17:59:00+0300

I'll see her next week and we'll go over both tests, and we'll review what Dzugan updates. should be interesting to see where we're going to go.

So not only was the consult worth every penny to me, it was worth every penny to my PCP - who learned new ways to look at things... this helped me, and its helping my PCP become a better doctor, than she already was. now that's priceless

so here's your master card commercial for the day

Educational consult w/Dr Kruse =$150, educating yourself and your PCP so you can fix your health = priceless.

### tweety — 2012-07-09T20:36:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36364>

I agree with Shijin. It is definitely worth every penny, and then some! I have been following Dr. Kruse for a long time, and I learned a ton from my own consult. But the biggest benefit to me was when I convinced my husband to have a consult. I have been sharing everything I have learned with him for the last year, and making suggestions for him to try. He heard me, sometimes he tried the suggestions; sometimes he didn't. But for him to have talked with Dr. Kruse, and heard it all straight from him... that was the jolt he needed. In one week he has made several changes and already seeing the benefits. His enthusiasm and committment are now completely there, and he sees his health as a priority and a gift. My husband has always been motivated, and a hard worker- just never with his health. Well now he is, and there is nothing that he won't achieve now.

So, I cannot thank Dr. Kruse enough. I am going to have my husband around for a long, happy, healthy life thanks to him. Our children are going to have a father around for many years to come. Theres nothing more I can say, nothing more worthwhile than that. So thank you Dr. Kruse for giving me my husband back.

From reading here since 9/2011 I had so much info, opinions, advice, experiences,etc. swimming around in my head, but no ability to sort it out for me specifically, and I haven't had recent labs done because I wasn't sure which ones would be helpful. I've wasted much $$ in the past for labs that didn't help and countless supplements, hoping something I'd read about would take care of my issues.

I left the consult with everything sorted out and with specific steps to take that are for my issues in particular. No more guessing. I should've done it sooner, but I'm hopeful now that my labs are ordered and my pharmacy & dr. are chosen.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-10T00:53:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36408>

I had a consult on Sunday. The first thing I can say is that I am very impressed by Dr. K's ability to sort through so much information from so many people during his consultation marathon, stay cheerful and help so many people in such a short time. He gave me a fantastic amount of information to take away and like others have mentioned...it feels inspiring to have this kind of knowledge and direction after years of ping ponging around.

My 23andme results came up after he had looked at most of my records and there was a bit of a "Bingo" moment when he told me that my whole history made sense in light of the risk factors I had for macular degeneration....in other words my mission is to get my circadian light cycles really, really tight and build up my DHEA/cortisol axis which will then work on my leaky gut...bone degeneration, immunity issues and energy. My family background told him that I started with some bad epigenetic patterns and unless I get myself together I'm going to end up like my parents (who didn't have any idea of what made them so ill) ...likewise my two daughters didn't get an optimal start, so they are going to be educated along with me. It was a real live demonstration of the way "light trumps everything" and how epigenetics are speeding up the frequency and timing of neolithic disease in my family.

What made sense to him makes sense to me too now ...I got out of hormone balance at a very young age because of a high degree of sensitivity to light. The cascade of issues that follows simply stems from this basic mismatch, so all my "disparate" issues are actually all stemming from one core issue. Soooo, the good news is my prescription involves managing my light well, diving into the full epipaleo diet to keep visual pathways in prime condition, supplementing as soon as I can tolerate the supplements, and increasing oxytocin EVERY DAY!

These consultations are seriously worth doing. There is a lot of power that comes from knowing what is the most important aspect of your health to focus on. I've done a huge amount of work, research experimenting, etc. but I would never have put my eyes and circadian biology in first place and now that I have, I can start to undo the damage.

### Jude — 2012-07-10T03:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36441>

I also had my consult on Sunday- Dr K's very messy last. Apparently **none** of my emails had arrived with my history and minimal labs.! I was late because I was so nervous, I kept dialing wrong numbers into the access code- took me nearly 5mins and DH's intervention -yes 5mins -to get it right! Poor Dr K. However, he soldiered on with absolutely no preparation from me and a very muddled phone history. Was it worth it?

Absolutely.He focused me and advised the necessary pathology & treatment strategies to implement the changes necessary for me.

What I really appreciated was the general discussion/education around the health of my grown children and how to start the damage control now. I will have another consult. I will learn how to use skype. I will learn how to record it. I will learn how to send a pdf file.

My family is so excited that that I actually did something like this. Ring a Dr in America from Australia--unheard of..... I never stop quoting Dr Kruse and now I've actually spoken to him....like wow! I needed to do this for me but just as importantly I needed to do it for my family's future health. And for that, None of us can ever be grateful enough To Dr. Kruse for his generous giving of time and his God given talents to help the rest of us poor mortals!! THANKS DR KRUSE.

### steve@wity.net — 2012-07-10T17:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36552>

I had a consult with Jack at the back end of April. At that point I was suffering pretty badly with adrenal fatigue and the foggy brain was unbearable. My hormones were low across the board. Here's some of them to give you a glimpse of where you don't want to be..i'm male, age 47. Jack likes to see these in the top 25% of the reference range.

Pregnenolone 122 ng/Dl (30-350)

Progesterone 0.31 ng/ml

DHEAs 130 ug/Dl (170-670) sleep as you can guess was pretty poor....

DHEA 270 ng/Dl (180-1250)

Aldosterone 2.48 ng/Dl (>15.894) this was so low it was retested by the lab...closely related to cortisol.

DHT 20.29 ng/Dl (9.39-47.59)

Cortisol had flat lined. Free testosterone low. Had very low magnesium along with high calcium. Copper was way up due to stress and zinc too low. Potassium and sodium both low. My thyroid ratio was 11 indicating Leptin resistance. Ferritin was way up.

The only two bits of good news were that I didn't have thyroid antibodies and that my hsCRP had come in at 0.38! Jack told me I need to aim for 0.03...

I just sat and listened to Jack and made copius notes. I was given a lot of specific things to do take - Liposomal Glutathione (IV Glutathione is hard to come by here in the UK but that would have been first choice), NAC, high dose Vitamin C via citrus (better absorbed), DHEA, Mag Threonate, K2, CoQ10 (ubiquinol), Betaine HCL. Bone broths were to be on the menu. Raw cream and Kefir as well. Epsom salt baths for magnesium. Along with this lot I was to do the Leptin RX and start CT. I was warned that I would strugle with the big early breakfasts - I did. I was told to start with high temperature CT and work my way down with what I could tolerate. I suffer from cold hands and feet as my system shut down and so the thought of getting into a cold bath filled me with dread.

Anyway I decided I would do things in stages. I started with all the supplements first and began to feel better within two weeks. I then introduced high temp CT wearing hat, socks and gloves - feet and hands not in the water. Started at 80F and I am currently down to 65F. I adjusted to the breakfasts over a 4 week period and then started the Leptin RX proper. So 10 weeks supplementation, 6 weeks of Leptin Rx and 7 weeks high temp CT - where am I?

Brain fog has cleared

Sleep is way better.

Energy levels picking up nicely.

Skin is softer.

I really only have weight to lose around my middle but I am seem to be altering shape.

Skin is getting darker even from my high temp CT (I tan easily)

The black swollen bags under my eyes are lightening and reducing in size.

Libido had returned when I first started taking the DHEA but seems to have faded again when i introduced the Leptin RX and CT.

Digestion and BM's a lot better.

So all in all from not being a "very happy camper" I'm feeling much better. If you can do a consult with Jack - do it.

I have another consult at the weekend to see if I need to change anything and discuss my 23andme results which are in. Going to be interesting!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-11T14:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36783>

> **vkiernan;26665 wrote:** Had my 30 min. consult with Dr. K. today and it was no question worth every penny as souldanzer said. I only sent labs as I had no idea I should write up a history. That would have helped tremendously to put more pieces together; but, even though he didn't have that he still gave me great advice and a plan of action. I know what to work on first. I will not hesitate to do it again especially after I start my plan with a new doc. to make sure I am on track. I even have a plan until I find a new doc. Things aren't pretty in my arena either but they can also be fixed.

Had my 2nd consult last Friday and this time it was all about the history that I didn't send the 1st time. I can tell you that history is soooo important that it changed my 2nd consult results. Things that you would think would not be significant are. It even changed the order of things I have to work on. But I am happy about that since my hormones are whacked and I can put them up front now instead of at the rear to work on. I'm sure my dh will be happy about that too. I still have to find that great doc locally but I will persevere.

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-12T00:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36975>

So I had my consult with Dr. Jack last Friday, five days ago, and I'm still absorbing it. He shook me! He said I have to do extraordinary things to "turn this Titanic around." First, read every Suzanne Somers book. Okay, that's pretty extraordinary right there, LOL. Kidding, Dr. Jack! I'm now reading "Breakthrough: Eight Steps to Wellness," and I love it. I keep taking notes.

Next he said to talk to my doctor about considering to get on Bioidentical Hormone Replacement immediately. This surprised me, because the anti-aging doc I saw when I got my cancer diagnosis said it's iffy with an estrogen-related cancer. But I was already wanting to do this in the belief that balancing out my hormones will strengthen my body. I had labs done yesterday, and hopefully will get this BHRT ball rolling quickly now.

He said to consider doing ocean CT seven days a week. Working on that one. I've improved the routine so I can now drive there, swim, and get back home in around 2 hours. This morning I ate my BAB, did my ocean CT, walked the dog, and meditated all by 9:30 a.m. Why, the day's just starting by then. Plenty of time to get everything else done.

He also said that for me, circadian rhythm is crucial. I've been using my blue-blocking glasses already, but now I'm more committed to using them at the crack of sunset and bringing them with me if I go out. He said that both my cancers were epithelial, which is closely tied to light cycles, and that my childhood visions problems contributed to the mismatch. I don't quite understand how, but okay. I've been waking up at dawn and going to sleep early for weeks now, and I adore it.

Next: eat seafood, especially shellfish. This is new for me. I'd been eating fish, but not shellfish. So I stocked up on canned crab and clams and frozen shrimp and scallops at Trader Joe's. Thank god for TJs. Cheap coconut oil and shellfish!

Oh, and he told me i should consider having two orgasms a day. Not every day a doc has prescribed that for me. But I need the oxytocin for my brain as the reason to help my brain inflammation. This one is of course enjoyable, although I've actually found it harder to have an orgasm lately. Hoping the BHRT will help with that.

I think those are the main points. He made them quite emphatically for me to consider. He said I was a doctor's worst nightmare! I've got a lot going on, for sure.

Thank you, Dr. Jack! And btw, I'm up for all this. It's all do-able. I'm looking forward to seeing and feeling improvements.

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-12T00:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36982>

I now fully understand why Dr. Jack encourages you to have a good physician to help you with results, etc.

I have to admit to operator error... I gave Dr. Jack all my lab results for my consult thinking they were right...well...when I saw my PCP yesterday I relayed his concern about my estradiol number...she said Carol we've never done an estradiol on you....apparently I had a total estrogen not an estradiol. No wonder he was concerned. I had my blood drawn this morning and I should have new results soon along with everything else he suggested.

I'm so bummed that my recording of my consult didn't work I would love to be able to listen to it until again and again. Won't make that mistake again.

So get your consult..you won't be disappointed...I can't wait until I have everything together fot the next one!

### roselvr — 2012-07-12T00:48:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-36988>

I just had my first consult on Sunday afternoon. I was able to record the meeting, thank you Gretchen!, and have spent the last 4 days combing through the information and recommendations he provided. I am extremely happy with how the meeting went and all that I learned!! I immediately started implementing some of the educational recommendations he made and have been working on a plan for implementing others as soon as possible. One of the BEST things about speaking with Dr. Kruse was him telling me that everything in my history and labs makes sense; that he understands why I have been a "difficult" case for health care providers; and that I am NOT crazy...my hormones are just a wreck from all of the crap I have been through! He is the ONLY doctor who has ever seen the big picture and put ALL of the pieces together in my health story. He says I will get better if I do what he educated me upon, and I will do it and am doing it! Once I am well on my way to optimal, my husband is next!

Rosekelly, you and I had nearly identical consults but that's not surprising given we both have a cancer history. I'm reading Knockout at the moment; Breakthrough is next! ### Beestly — 2012-07-17T03:28:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-38588>

Hi everyone, I am helping Jack put together some testimonials for his first book and he would like to include some consultation experiences. A couple of people here shared some specifics about what they learned about their health and what their treatment protocol consisted of.

I think readers will really connect to the material when they can hear about what you learned from your labs (the good and not so good), how everything connects, including with your history, and what sort of specific things you are doing to improve your health.

Also, has anything improved yet? If so, include that too!

You can either share here or you can message me if you want to keep it private. We do not have to use your real name in the book, you can stay totally anonymous.

I haven't tried messaging on this forum yet but I'm assuming it works. I hope you can help! I personally love reading real-life experiences, I think readers will too. It will make the information more useable I think.

Thanks!

Elaine

### Mery — 2012-07-19T22:57:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-39476>

I'm having a consult with Dr Jack and I'd like to record on skype however i don't know how to do it? help

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-19T23:17:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-39477>

> **Mery;39662 wrote:** I'm having a consult with Dr Jack and I'd like to record on skype however i don't know how to do it? help

do you have a PC or MAC?

for those wanting to record a skype call w/Jack

for PCs: [http://www.vodburner.com/](http://http://www.vodburner.com/)

for Macs: [http://www.ecamm.com/mac/callrecorder/](http://http://www.ecamm.com/mac/callrecorder/)

I used ecamm - it was very easy... then I edited the entire call into segments that were less than 15mins using iMovie, then I converted it to a quicktime video so I could upload to my youtube pages, as unlisted - basically provides a link - and only those ppl w/a link can view the video.

both of these should have a pop up that once installed on your computer as a widget of skype - will allow you to record...

### fitness@home — 2012-07-30T03:56:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-41890>

My consult experience, what to say…

This was one of the best things I have done for myself this year in regards to my health. Another important part was the testing and lab work (and that includes the out-of-pocket expenses too). My doctor ran a lot of the standard tests for me and those were important, but the Genova Complete Hormone, ZRT Dinural Salivary Cortisol, and 23andme tests added much depth to the consult.

The initial test results are essential in determining a baseline for comparison down the road. I want to know I am making progress.

Found out my absolute biggest issue is CORTISOL and I must get this under control first and foremost. Circadian rhythm will be very important in my healing process. Many other issues will begin to fall into place as the cortisol is corrected and then I can start working my way through the rest.

My early AM and Noon cortisol levels are off the charts, which reflects just how much stress is going on during the course of my day. Crazy how you can just get used to excessive stressors in your life and think you are handling it all just fine. I did hear you loud and clear on this Doc. The wheels have been turning for the past 24 hours.

I am going to repeat some of what has already been said. When writing your medical history, include details even if you don’t think it is important or was from childhood. For example, I listed one detail from my life at eight years of age. He honed right in on it; as an indicator of things not functioning properly in my body even that long ago.

Another small detail that was snagged from my history was a craving for salt that occurs between 5-6 p.m. almost daily. This little detail correlates directly to my cortisol issue. Connecting the dots… I included medical, environmental, dental, maternal and paternal histories.

**For the ladies**, please consider sending a few pictures in with the other data you submit for your consult. Men will usually do this; but women rarely do. I sent in front/side/back pictures of myself in active wear. Things that Jack picked up on in my test results and medical history were _confirmed_ by the pictures.

I have come to terms with the fact that dairy products and eggs are out for me. The autoimmune protocol diet will be incorporated into my life. Not developing an autoimmune disease is far more important than my love for cheese!

My "To Do" list for tomorrow includes scheduling an appointment with a GOOD doctor. Going to take his advice and fire the current one.

### TerrierMom — 2012-08-13T02:18:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-44608>

Hard to do sometimes because if you are doing things for preventative reasons sometimes insurance doesn't pony up, unless it is for their recommended screenings (pap smear, mammogram, cholesterol, etc). So you end up on your own. If you can get a "diagnostic" code out of your doc, as in, they think you have something going on but need labs to be sure, then insurance can cover it.... But insurance billing is a nightmare so I'd be dodging the heck out of it if I were JK, you need a whole staff just to deal with it.

Just realize in the long run, it is cheaper to pony up for your own labs than to live a disease ridden life. If it were a choice between cutting out Direct tv and buying labs, I'd buy a cheap antenna and watch broadcast tv for a few months..... Priorities.... (or better yet, ditch it altogether, who needs all the artificial light after sunset anyway!!). And before anyone points it out, I know, there's folks who that's not their issue, they're barely paying the rent. But there are also those who have their morning lattes and hbo subscriptions who need to realize they CAN afford the labs if they rethought their spending.

I'm just sayin.... Rethink it.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-08-13T04:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-44619>

I did mine as a timeline, with objective/subjective data from critical times of my life. Used Nutrition, Mood, Physical, Medical, Stress as bullet points

started with birth weight, birth order, breast fed 2 yrs, etc then in 5-10 year jumps roughly. Made for a lengthy write up, but it was very enlightening for me to write it. I included things like how long I had consumed diet soda, how many years on birth control, what my weight was over the years, exercise history, surgical history, etc. HTH

### diane — 2012-08-14T03:22:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-44820>

I did my first consult on Sunday morning, and I wish I would have gone through this thread first! Specifically the advice to record it - I was taking mad notes, but love the idea of reviewing the consult multiple times. There is so much information - at one point, we had covered so much ground that I though a half hour must have passed - and it was only 15 minutes! Imagine what the next 45 minutes were like. So amazing.

I had some labs from a year ago, some from a few months ago. My immediate next step is to find an anti-aging doctor (thanks for the tips in the webinar!) and then get new labs done. My issue is leaky gut which is causing so many other issues. So I'm following the circadian rhythms, eating epi-paleo (did some amazing seafood shopping yesterday and made fabulous scallops for dinner), with some supplements to start healing the gut. Because I live in Canada, I don't have access to all the same supplements as the US, but he gave me some fabulous resources and people to get in touch with to help out - so thank you for helping me make the connections!

The consult itself was so full of helpful information. I love Dr. Kruse's style - it's direct and to the point, no nonsense, and he makes you accountable for yourself. At one point, I did have a few tears (not easy for me to admit), not because he was harsh, but because he was right. Right that there are going to be bigger problems if I don't fix things now, and right that I've really been hurting myself by not getting on this and sticking to it. It was very motivational for me, and I've actually made changes in terms of when I eat my meals, and making sure I go to bed earlier.

I have so much to learn, but I'm really committed to following through. And yes, I've downloaded the Suzanne Somers book too! LOL.

Thank you so much Dr. Kruse - and I look forward to talking to you again in my next consult!

### fitness@home — 2012-08-14T16:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-44894>

Some post-consult feedback hereâ€¦

In just two weeks there has been a significant change in how deeply I am sleeping. It feels so good to wake up well-rested instead of tired.

Having the consult was just a step toward reclaiming my health. _Implementing_ the suggestions from the consult is whatâ€™s generating the change. Iâ€™m not sure which of the following is having such a dramatic effect on the quality of my sleep - maybe it is a combination of them.

Based on my consult I implemented the following changes:

\- Get morning sunlight on my retinas. I now stop at a park on the way to work and watch the sunrise for about 20 minutes. Kick off my sandals and plant my bare feet on the ground (tip: always survey area for fire ants first!)

\- Meditation â€“ Use those 20 minutes to visualize healing and changes going on in my body and also enjoy the nature all around me

\- Added Magnesium L-Threonate

\- Added DHEA cream in the AM

\- Weaning myself from coffee. Down to drinking one very weak cup per day (previously 3-4)

\- Dropped dairy and eggs from diet

\- Work stress â€“ Had IT remove Lotus Notes from my iPad. Itâ€™s too easy to stay tethered to work these days, especially when you work for a global company. No more responding to emails in the evening, weekends, or at 4 AM!

So if you are hesitating about scheduling a consult for any reason, just do it! Take the plungeâ€¦you are worth it.

### TerrierMom — 2012-08-15T21:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-45064>

@Nate R I did mine and covered major areas- it is really hard to say what to write because we all have different issues

Started with a 2-line history on my parents, grandparents, sisters (i,e, overweight or not, cancers, smokers, what they died from, how long lived).

Then covered my Weight history (how long heavy, major ups.downs) married to diet history (the way I achieved the ups and downs), my surgeries (if any, i.e. do you have a gallbladder?), Bones (if issues), teeth, skin, sleep, adrenal issue potential, stuff like that. What supplements I currently took. Medications. Plus CONSULT GOALS and QUESTIONS.

Also after you write up your history PRINT IT OUT to have it handy during your consult (I didn't!). And, use vodburner to record your Skype session because you will want to watch it again and get more out of it! I tried to listen more than I took notes but at one point Dr Kruse told me to put down my pen and listen to the next bit so I would get it ! (so I was not entirely successful!).

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-08-18T19:16:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-45562>

I am extremely pleased with the progress I have made since my 1st consult back in June. As many people have said, Dr. Kruse quickly zeroes in on the key areas. One comment he made I will never forget, "your labs are similar to a younger Crossfitter". Ouch, exercise was an important part of my life.

Dr. Kruse said I had a cortisol / thyroid issue … and it was caused by too much exercise. He suggested that I work out a max of 4 days / week (instead of my usual 7), and stop doing all cardio immediately. Wow … I needed a major life style change, but I was paying for his expertise in my quest for Optimal … so I changed my workout routine immediately. I tried to justify several points about my workouts, but Dr. Kruse politely but bluntly said "your labs say otherwise." Instinctively I knew he was correct.

The life style changes also impacted my diet. No problem eating clean since I have been grain free for over 2 years, but he wanted me to give up 2 of my favorite foods; bacon and dark chocolate. Instead, Dr. Kruse suggested eating lots of seafood. (unbeknownst to me, I was getting the epi-paleo Rx before it was released).

We reviewed my supplements and he suggested deleting all of my B vitamins along with tyrosine, 5-HTP, and SAMe. Finally, Dr. Kruse said my night owl life was negatively impacting my circadian rhythms. Get to bed earlier and start wearing blue blocker goggles a couple of hours before bed. Lots of valuable info in my 1st consult … now I had to implement the many changes.

My 2nd consult was in July. I had positive changes to report. Most surprising to me was that despite eliminating B vitamins along with other supplements, my mental fog was slowly lifting, and the amount of negative thoughts had greatly decreased. Dr. Kruse said that was the result of my eating at least 1/2 pound of nutrient rich seafood every day.

He was still very concerned with my adrenals, and thought I was in stage 3 adrenal fatigue. Dr. Kruse was worried that my adrenals might not recover. Hopefully the protocol would work, but I needed new labs to measure my progress. Prior to my next consult, I would have a 4x cortisol saliva panel, thyroid panel, and hormones panel.

Last week, I had my 3rd consult with Dr. Kruse. Great news … my adrenals have healed a great deal; still room for improvement, but they are healing. Still no cardio, but hopefully in the 3 months or so I can resume it. Good news is that I can resume eating dark chocolate. Overall, my labs are improving and Dr. Kruse thinks if my improvements continue that my thyroid, cortisol and hormone issues may be resolved before winter. Optimal here I come.

In summary, I am very thankful for the opportunity to consult with Dr. Kruse. I started the Leptin Rx in July 2011 and made a fair amount of progress. Since my 1st consult this June, the progress has continued, and now seems to be accelerating. After my last consult, I KNOW that optimal is achievable. My advice includes several key points:

1) start the process with a consult (I waited too long for my 1st consult and thus wasted time and money)

2) do the work and make the changes that Dr. Kruse recommends

3) have positive faith in the process … it works!

4) understand that most people will need multiple consults and multiple labs spread out of a period of months (at least)

5) the process works … Dr. Kruse is by far the most knowledgeable person I know regarding the topic Optimal Health

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-18T23:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-45609>

I did my first consult this morning, and now, 4 hours later, it still weight heavily in my mind. I have Hashimoto's (for 13 years) and have experienced a real downward turn in my health in the past 2 years, and steepening over the last several months. I had been patiently waiting for another consult "sale", but decided it was time when I got notice that prices were going to be increasing!

As so many others have described, Dr Kruse really zeroed in on urgency of my condition. It still echoes in my mind when he said that Hashi's uncouples the body's hormones from the brain. A loss of control at the brain level is causing me all these symptoms (insomnia, fatigue, easy weight gain off the diet, low blood pressure, elevated heartrates, the endless desire to be eating (tied to low brain dopamine)). He re-itereated the need to continue with a strict epi-paleo diet (allready under way for several months), supplements to support the gut and their dosing (which should eventually help hormone levels and control). We also spent a lot of time discussing metformin, and cycloset. I have had a great deal of labwork done, but I need to do it more systematically, on a quarterly basis to track my progress. Quarterly DHEA, ASI, LE female comprehensive panel, thyroid numbers. He also reitereated the urgency of getting my 4 children on the diet, since my placenta didn't do the job it needed to do to protect them from these conditions (2 of my 4 have Hashi's starting at age 3). And, he reitereated the connection to factor X - epigenetics speeding up for each generation. He predicted their symptoms will be more severe than mine as they progress in this "dynamic" disease.

Dr. Kruse' blogs and forum have given me a plethora of resources, and were really critical to diving into the meat of my particulars. But addressing my particular issues needs a one-on-one approach. I finished the consult with specific lab goals, diet goals, supplements to address my needs, and things to research further (like listening to the hormones webinar, finding out more about T3 mostly thyroid replacement). It was a very worthy experience, to say the least!

Finally, I am afraid I didn't come across very friendly, or grateful during our talk. I was both overwhelmed with the information coming my way, and a little nervous too! Dr. Kruse, if you read this post, I say with the utmost sincerity that I am so grateful for your generosity in sharing so much through your blogs, webinars, forums, and then offering us individual attention through consultation.

### caroline — 2012-08-19T11:25:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-45667>

Thankyou to everyone who is sharing their consult experiences. It is a massive revelation how important it is to get a one on one consult to address our individual problems and concerns and goals and it is great to hear about the progress you are making.

### nate.reik@yahoo.com — 2012-08-20T03:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-45782>

My wife and I had our first consults today.

We had no labs at all. My thought was to not waste time and money getting useless labs, or not getting labs that would be helpful, so we had a consult FIRST, and then will get labs NEXT. Jack commented that we were the first people in our age group to do a consult before labs, and he thought it was a good idea. After doing it, I agree, he told me I should think about some labs I hadn't even considered would apply to me.

As has been mentioned, he went right into the main issues. I had a hunch what mine were, and he seemed to feel the same, and identified other potential ones I hadn't considered. Was good in some ways to get that reinforcement.

He made some guesses about my wife's mother's history that resulted in issues in my wife. My wife called her mother after the consult and turns out that Jack was right. This was something my wife wasn't even aware of, and wasn't a really obvious link to most people.

So, we'll see where it goes, but I just felt it needed to be reinforced that it's worth it to consult first, then labs.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-23T00:18:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-46422>

Oh happy day! The consults have done the trick!

Earlier this year I did the Leptin reset, which greatly improved sleep. Then I tried CT, which gave me some nice muscle improvements and some fat loss. Then I stopped CT for the Summer and picked up seafood, and experienced big mental and skin and vision benefits. Then Dr. Kruse analyzed the lab tests for me, and he suggested some changes. I instituted most of them, including resuming CT, earlier than I had planned.

Wow! It’s only been 1.5 weeks of CT, 7 cold baths, and I’m seeing results already! And DH has even noticed. According to my scale, in 7 days I’ve lost 3 lbs, body fat went down 4%, and muscle went up 2%. My thighs, tush, belly and upper arms are shrinking! I am so excited! And I feel so good! Thank you so much, Dr. K!

### NWgirl — 2012-09-05T02:52:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-49502>

I had my first consult yesterday, and wow was I stunned! It was only a half hour but went very well. Received more info during that time than years put together.

Jack... if you're reading this... please note that when you logged into my 23andme account it defaulted to my Korean daughter's results not mine. Dumb 23andme design! Not sure why it wouldn't default to me. So I'm definitely 100% northern european.

I learned that I became morbidly obese due to gut flora and possible hashimoto's, not because I'm a weak willed and lazy person. Sure wish I had received this info 20 years ago when I was in my early 20's and really struggling. My TSH was over 4 and no doctor would ever even do other thyroid tests on me. I had to struggle just to have them give me thyroid meds.

One thing that scared me is that Jack said that before I started leptin RS a few months ago that I was on the fast track to one of those silent but fatal heart attacks. I love how when my workplace runs cholesterol tests on me they are like wow you are so healthy, what a low value! Ignorant medical professionals don't even know that a low number can be deadly.

I will be looking into hashi's, metformin and doing all of my testing again in about a month and then having a consult. I need to see that I'm making improvement with the current things that I'm doing - sticking to leptin RS, iodine supplementation mostly.

Not sure how to improve my gut flora at this point, but I'm hoping that in time, all will be revealed to me.

thanks so much Jack for a great consult...

Kim

### mafixit — 2012-09-07T03:31:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-49900>

I am considering a consult but want to know, how do I know which labs to get? My Mayo Clinic PCP may agree to order them so I can get covered under insurance. The nice thing about Mayo is everything is on line and downloadable, including the doctor notes in detail for every visit.

I have to call UHC and ask them how they handle labs that I ask for, or can I myself even ask a lab for them?

We have good insurance and I am up to my deductible for the year already thanks to all the tests and specialist visits, so maybe I can get labs covered. Also if Dr. Jack is listed under UHC, I might be able to get reimbursement.

Is it optimal for me to not be on my birth control pills prior to labs? Or will it matter? I was on for 30 years (I am 48)- only off long enough to have my daughter, then off for two months in June/July because of the migraine diagnosis. I crashed and burned when all my internal girly parts woke up, so they put me back on 2 weeks ago. OBGYN took labs and said I was 'perimenopausal'.

Karen

Karen, I'd guess if you want hormonal testing you need to be off the pill. But (and this is unsolicited advice, but nonetheless I am screaming it from the rooftops), you need to get off the birth control pills right away!!! If you have migraines or hormonal issues, this is the way to make sure you get cancer trying to correct it.

You can also have a consult before you get labs and Dr K can suggest the best labs for you. The 'down and dirty' 3 to start with are hsCRP (not regular CRP), DHEA-S and Vitamin D. There is also a blog on labs you can read. I am not in the US, but from my understanding, there are MANY labs you can access yourself.

email UCH with the lists of labs Jack Gives you during the email consult he mentioned. ask them if they are covered. Most likely they'll say yes, as long as they are drawn at Lab corp and you have a DX Code. then Ask them what your DX codes are. they're you're codes - so you have access to them, and should be able to use them... have your PCP fill out a chit, w/DX codes - you should have at least 90% coverage of all labs if not 100%.

My DH had his first consultation w/ Dr. Kruse on Sunday. DH is a very practical, left-brained, prove it to me person. He loves TV at night and the orange glasses have always pissed him off because he can't see the remote or the glass of water (knocked one over the other night). The best part is that Dr. K didn't even bring up circadian living but he did stress the shellfish! Dr. K hit on a couple of points that rang true for DHs issues so it convinced DH that Jack was surely onto something. DH even took his first ice bath. Whoa! The best part is he loves to talk about health/food stuff to people. He will be spreading Krusology very soon. He even asked me to print out the current blog that Jack suggested he read. He is a convert! I'm so happy!

### BJK77 — 2012-10-13T23:42:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consultation-experiences.1115/post-58010>

Another great consult with Jack today. I didn't have any new labs, so I just used the time to get his input on my new anti-aging doctor's approach and discuss a few other things that have been on my mind. It was fantastic just to chat and feel encouraged that we're on the right path.

After literally being a walking zombie only a few years ago I'm realizing just how far I've come. I did the GAPS diet for nearly 2 years before finding Dr. Kruse with very little success. Though I realize healing is not a linear process and I definitely still have my down moments (physically, mentally and emotionally), I can honestly say overall my health is trending upward and I feel so confident for the first time EVER that some day this will all be behind me and I'll be sharing my journey with others who need help.

After the consult I immediately went to the store and bought 2 dozen oysters! I even got my daughter to eat some for the first time - bribing her with the pearl I found in one was all it took

# Thread: Topical Menthol Thermogenesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-25T05:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-18613>

Got thinking about pathways, thought there might be another trigger of same afferent nerve... dug... Menthol... Dug further... Found a study, which confirms activation:

Tajino et al. (2011) recently reported that **topical menthol application or cold temperature** led to an increase in core body temperature that was dependent on TRPM8 expression and that TRPM8 expression was correlated with cold-induced UCP expression in BAT. However, for the first time, our study has demonstrated TRPM8 expression in brown adipocytes and has revealed a molecular pathway for TRPM8-dependent UCP1 expression. Although specific β3-adrenergic agonists can induce BAT activity and can activate thermogenesis in rodents, no metabolic effects of this agent were observed in a double-blinded, placebo-controlled randomized clinical study (Redman et al., 2007). Acute activation of TRPM8 induces both BAT and non-BAT-mediated heat-gain responses (Tajino et al., 2007). The present study cannot completely exclude some non-BAT-mediated effects in mice on long-term menthol diet.

[http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full](http://http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full)

CT without the C. MT...

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-05-02T00:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-20544>

After searching for any possible side effects or contraindications and finding nothing of note, I have tried this n=1 on myself.

Now that I am cold adapted (temp goes up in cold consistently), I wanted to see if menthol increased my core temperature and was therefore tricking my body into thinking it was thermally cold by activating the same nerve/response.

I hit some of torso (maybe 50% coverage) with a 15% menthol solution applied as a mist. Cold sensation was continuous for approximately 30 mins. Internal temp went up during (a few degrees more than normal) and mild shivering ensued about 10 mins after the cold sensation went away. Same to somewhat stronger response than ice tub ct.

Much less bearable than a 40-50 deg bath, as there was not the almost immediate numbness that I get now in the bath. Fairly uncomfortable, but not enough to deter me from repeating.

Baths are easier (less of the cold sensation) and more enjoyable, but this certainly thus far seems to be a valid way to get more CT while out and about. My n=1 so far shows similar response of sensory (menthol ligand) and thermal based TRPM8 activation.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-05-02T21:33:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-20771>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;20810 wrote:** where did you get your menthol? Did you just use peppermint oil?

The cheapest drugstore label brand of icy hot had only glycerin, menthol, alcohol and water as ingredients. Some have lots of fragrances and other ingredients in them.

Avoid anything with camphor as it will activate the heat receptor.

You can get bulk menthol crystals from amazon to make your own.

I'd assume that mint or similar oils would work as well but they are much more expensive.

I'll say again though, this is more uncomfortable than water, and you cant just "get out", you have to wait for it to unbind.

This not a replacement, as it is not a natural thing we'd find in our environment. Maybe someday we will have it all worked out, but for now we should be mimicking nature.

It may very well be that this can desensitize your cold receptors.

But if you try it at your risk and volition, post results here so we can gather more data.

### Entelechy — 2012-05-02T21:56:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-20786>

hmmm... reading this paper, they aren't applying it topically... they are feeding it to the mice, and a lot of it.

"There is a lot of data strongly suggesting that TRPM8 may have diverse biological functions outside of the peripheral nervous system."

e.g. "A [role for TRPM8 in either normal or cancerous prostate tissues is still unclear, and studies using models of prostate cancer, such as LNCaP cells, have shown that the channel does respond to cold and menthol in these cells \[43\]. However, **the channel is not present on the cell surface but is trapped in intracellular membrane compartments** such as the endoplasmic reticulum. This expression is androgen dependent, suggesting that TRPM8 may play a role in differentiating these cells](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK5238/)"

Plays a role in cell differentiation... eek. :-S

it would be great to find a study showing topical application is sufficient to induce thermogenesis.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-05-03T00:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-20839>

> **Big Bobcat;20911 wrote:** Would something like Vick vapo-rub (with menthol) be appropriate for this?

Look at the ingredients, a lot of the rubs and ointments have camphor (which activates the heat receptor). As long as the ingredients are menthol, and some inactive solvents/carriers like (alcohol/glycerol/water) it should work. But do this with caution, you can't just wipe it off.

If I remember correctly, menthol alone also potentiates 2 heat activated TRP's as well.

edit.... found it.

"human physiological studies demonstrate a para-

doxical role of menthol in modulation of warm sensation, and here, we

show that menthol also activates heat-activated TRPV3. We further

show that menthol inhibits TRPA1, potentially explaining the use of

menthol as an analgesic. Similar to menthol, both camphor and

cinnamaldehyde (initially reported to be specific activators of TRPV3

and TRPA1, respectively) also modulate other thermoTRPs.

I have found icing the knee for about an hour twice a day helps more than anything else for relieving the pain, and I think improving the problem. ### jihad4o — 2012-07-11T22:32:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-36923>

anyone thought of buying menthol crystals and mixing them up? The cheapest place to get menthol crystals is EBay so far(search for the one dispatched from the UK in 50/100g bottles). It should be soluble in oils(coconut jumps to mind, but I'm not sure if it'll work) and alcohols(since someone mentioned that ethanol is not suitable because it blocks the cold receptor, the guys from the tobacco forum hinted that we can use propylen glycol with high success). I have ordered the crystals, but we'll have to wait for delivery before I can share more details.

Also in CT-6 I read:

> Human cold receptors: (GEEKS) Cold adaptation takes 2 weeks in humans. It is mediated by surface skin receptors and not the deeper core cold receptors. This is the cold sensory afferent loop The cold receptor is called TMRP8 (transient receptor potential melastatin-8 channel) and is a menthol receptor that is a calcium/sodium voltage gated channel.

so I guess that menthol can be used _instead_ of cold to drive the adaptation, but we should be careful about its limitations. For example you can't stop it once it is on your skin and your core temperature is probably going up(the body is perceiving cold and creates more heat).

### lohd2015 — 2017-03-25T01:31:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-211033>

> [Matty\_M said:](/goto/post?id=210981)
>
> This thread is way old. But better late than never.
>
> Do you guys think there could be any harm from regularly using something like Tiger balm to help relax tense muscles? My inclination says that yoga, stretching, massage, CT, and sunlight are all better approaches, but maybe this is another hack to add.
>
> In other words, what could be the limitations to topically applied menthol? Where were you set a limit before diminishing returns

I would be cautious using Tiger Balm and other similar OTC products from Asia. The main ingredient in these formulas is camphor. The camphor used is usually white camphor (but you never can be sure....) Camphor is rapidly absorbed from the skin and [gastrointestinal](http://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/page/Gastrointestinal) tract, and toxic effects can occur within minutes of exposure. Chronic ingestion can result in hepatotoxicity and neurotoxicity. And as I always say, don't put anything on your skin unless you can eat it also.

Eucalyptus globulus is analgesic and anti-inflammatory.
I personally prefer wild-crafted Eucalyptus citriodora from Brazil. It is analgesic, sedative and anti-hypertensive. AND, mosquitoes hate it.

In aromatherapy, synergy is very powerful and important. I would combine Eucalyptus with a few other oils. What to use depends totally on your N=1. I'd use either organic jojoba or coconut oil as carrier.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-25T03:29:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-211036>

Pine oils are important and it is why I live in a pine forest. Most of my members know how fond I am about pine tress in the Gulf South and why I recommend people move tp places with these trees and strong light? Do you want to know why from a quantum perspective? Oxidized terpenes which are found in pine trees have the following properties: They 1. Relaxing smooth muscles 2. Acting as cough preservatives 3. Relieving congestion 4. Acting as anti-inammatory agen 5. tsHaving disinfectant properties (bactericidal)Increasing Here is a biggie......5. HbO2 Increasing O2 arterial partial pressure 6. Increasing O2 tissue diffusion and 7. Increasing the redox system activity in mitochondria of animals. 8. They are water-soluble and can effect cells which are filled with WATER!!!! ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT OF THE VOLATILE TURPENTINE FRACTION The most volatile components of turpentine are two terpenes: alpha (α) and beta (β) pinenes. They are the dominant odorous compounds emitted by trees, shrubs, and grasses. In the lower troposphere, and depending on the weather conditions at the top of pine trees, these compounds can react with OH° radicals, ozone, NO3 radical and O2. Indeed, the electric field in the canopy atmosphere (at the upper-most level of the pine and spruce forests) is sufcient to produce electric discharges, particularly in stormy weather, or more generally, in wet weather, through which ozone (O3) and hydrogen peroxides (H2O2) are released. Ozone also forms during sunny weather, particularly in the summer and autumn . Reactions in both these conditions result in the generation of aerosols in the ultra particlle form as well as of peroxides (hydrogen peroxides and organic peroxides), carbon monoxide (CO), acid rains (starting from organic acids, of NO3 and SO42-), ozone, or oxidizing radicals, like the OH° radical. These particle forms simulate the Lenard effect we see in waterfalls. [https://www.researchgate.net/public...r\_volatile\_fraction\_a-\_and\_b-pinenes\_A\_review](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/41669083_The_essential_oil_of_turpentine_and_its_major_volatile_fraction_a-_and_b-pinenes_A_review)

I've got a set of 12 combination aromatherapy mixes. The lung one contains eucalyptus, lavender and pine. The kidney one is atlas cedarwood, Virginia cedarwood, pine, ginger and cinnamon leaf. Any thoughts on the combos?

### lohd2015 — 2017-03-25T14:51:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-211052>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=211046)
>
> I've got a set of 12 combination aromatherapy mixes. The lung one contains eucalyptus, lavender and pine. The kidney one is atlas cedarwood, Virginia cedarwood, pine, ginger and cinnamon leaf. Any thoughts on the combos?

Sue, I have worked with all the individual oils you mentioned, and they are staples in my collection (which means they are excellent oils with very practical and useful applications)
Pre-formulated combinations can be useful in a very general sense. There is no one-size-fit all formula, just like everything else. For you, especially, with your N=1.
What I used to do for people is to have them smell a collection of individual oils which I think may benefit them, and from that, their bodies will tell me which are the ones they need. The formula will also change as the person's body responds and evolves.
As for the particular combinations of the two you mentioned, the lung one is pretty straightforward. I do have a question, what is the subspecies of the Eucalyptus? I find that Eucalyptus Radiata to be most effective in boosting immunity of the respiratory system, as well as the lymphatic system. Also, organic oils are more therapeutic in general.
The formula for the kidneys is more complicated. It totally depends on what objective you are trying to achieve. Tonic, diuretic, detox? And of course, your N=1 will also drive the particular oils being selected.
The most important question for you: How does your body feel after using them? what is your method of delivery? Do you use a carrier oil or do you use inhalation? If inhalation, what type of diffuser do you use?

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-25T15:41:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/topical-menthol-thermogenesis.1118/post-211058>

> [lohd2015 said:](/goto/post?id=211052)

I find that Eucalyptus Radiata to be most effective in boosting immunity of the respiratory system, as well as the lymphatic system. Also, organic oils are more therapeutic in general.
> The formula for the kidneys is more complicated. It totally depends on what objective you are trying to achieve. Tonic, diuretic, detox? And of course, your N=1 will also drive the particular oils being selected.
> The most important question for you: How does your body feel after using them? what is your method of delivery? Do you use a carrier oil or do you use inhalation? If inhalation, what type of diffuser do you use?

Yes its Eucalyptus Radiata. The blends are organic, low temp and low pressure distilled. I have the 12 in a box, and pick intuitively, then give it a good sniff. My first massage therapist used to try to get me to smell and pick them, but in the end chose for me ..... It took me 6 months of weekly massage to realise that chamomile even had a smell .... These oils were originally only sold to practitioners for clinical use, and aren't available any more, but the mixes interest me. If you are interested I will type up the list and botanical names of the 12 and post it in my journal. I now intuitively pick, sniff as a second check, either breathe it in from that or put one drop on a wrist and rub my wrists together, breathing it in as well. My current massage therapist will use my pick if I ask her, but for massage I prefer what I call "raw massage" with a mix of the Bach flower remedies and some base oil. Without the distraction of scent, I "feel" my body more, (being glycated and past trauma makes me hyposensitive to physical touch), and a "raw massage" gives me more body awareness, rather than just living in my head.

# Thread: Ex-diabetic in legal trouble for advocating going paleo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ex-diabetic-in-legal-trouble-for-advocating-going-paleo.1122/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-04-25T15:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ex-diabetic-in-legal-trouble-for-advocating-going-paleo.1122/post-18668>

CHARLOTTE â€” The North Carolina Board of Dietetics/Nutrition is threatening to send a blogger to jail for recounting publicly his battle against diabetes and encouraging others to follow his lifestyle.

Chapter 90, Article 25 of the North Carolina General Statutes makes it a misdemeanor to â€œpractice dietetics or nutritionâ€ without a license. According to the law, â€œpracticingâ€ nutrition includes â€œassessing the nutritional needs of individuals and groupsâ€ and â€œproviding nutrition counseling.â€

Steve Cooksey has learned that the definition, at least in the eyes of the state board, is expansive.

When he was hospitalized with diabetes in February 2009, he decided to avoid the fate of his grandmother, who eventually died of the disease. He embraced the low-carb, high-protein Paleo diet, also known as the â€œcavemanâ€ or â€œhunter-gathererâ€ diet. The diet, he said, made him drug- and insulin-free within 30 days. By May of that year, he had lost 45 pounds and decided to start a blog about his success.

more here:

[http://www.carolinajournal.com/exclusives/display\_exclusive.html?id=8992](http://http://www.carolinajournal.com/exclusives/display_exclusive.html?id=8992)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ex-diabetic-in-legal-trouble-for-advocating-going-paleo.1122/post-18669>

# Thread: Beef Is Safe!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-25T16:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/post-18673>

USDA announced a positive test result on April 24, 2012, as part of its targeted surveillance program to test cattle for Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (BSE). USDAâ€™s Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS) has confirmed the nation's fourth case of bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE) in a dairy cow from central California. According to USDA, the carcass of the animal is being held under State authority at a rendering facility in California and will be destroyed. It was never presented for slaughter for human consumption, so at no time presented a risk to the food supply or human health.

The bottom line for consumers remains the same: all U.S. beef is safe. The BSE agent is not found in meat like steaks and roasts. It is only found in central nervous system tissue such as brain and spinal cord. Experts in human and animal health agree that U.S. beef is safe from BSE because of the multiple interlocking safeguards and progressive steps taken by the U.S. government over the past two decades. The worldâ€™s leading scientists, medical professionals and government officials agree that BSE is not a public or animal health risk in the U.S.

BSE is extremely rare in the U.S.

\* The U.S. started taking preventive steps against BSE in 1989. BSE is not a risk in this country because significant actions were taken well before there was an opportunity for this disease to take hold.

\* BSE can only be spread through contaminated feed and, in 1997, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), with the full support of the beef industry, banned from cattle feed such protein supplements that could spread BSE. BSE is not a contagious disease.

\* USDAâ€™s ongoing BSE surveillance program tests approximately 40,000 high-risk cattle annually, bringing the total of tested animals to more than 1 million since the program began. A scientific analysis of seven years of surveillance data found the estimated prevalence of BSE in the United States to be less than one infected animal per 1 million adult cattle.

\* The already low risk in this country, coupled with an effective feed ban supports the Harvard Center for Risk Analysis projections that, if BSE currently exists in the U.S., it is extremely rare.

Because of the strong measures in place, the risk of BSE in the U.S. is approaching zero.

USDA Public Health Veterinarians examine every single animal before processing and condemn those with any signs of illness. Animals most likely to have BSE are older animals either unable to walk or showing signs of neurological disease. Such animals are banned from the human food supply.

The strong BSE measures we have in place in the U.S. apply to all beef produced in this country. All beef, regardless of whether it is organic, natural or conventionally raised, is safe. No cattle in the U.S. can be fed animal by-products that could spread BSE.

The BSE surveillance program in the U.S. is designed to detect the disease even if the prevalence rate is as low as one case per 1 million adult cattle.

The BSE risk in this country is so low, in part, because of a progressive series of strong actions taken by the U.S. government. Actions such as removal of materials that would most likely carry BSE, banning animals that show signs of the neurological disease and the effective feed ban ensure the very low risk of BSE in the U.S.

For more information, [www.bseinfo.org](http://http://www.bseinfo.org)

[http://beefmagazine.com/biosecurity/key-messages-producers-bse?page=1](http://http://beefmagazine.com/biosecurity/key-messages-producers-bse?page=1)

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-25T16:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/post-18691>

Concerned about BSE? Why? According to the World Organization of Animal Health (OIE), the instances of BSE is almost completely eliminated worldwide. There have been 4 cases of BSE in the U.S. in the last nine years; it's VERY rare. In 2011, there was 29 cases of BSE worldwide.

Does our food supply need work in other areas? You bet. But, this is an issue of animal health, which might I add has been quickly contained, and is absolutely not an issue of food safety.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-25T18:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/post-18748>

> **freshveggies;18802 wrote:** I don't think it is that rare. My dad died of this diseas 2 1/2 years ago. We know it is not genetic from testing, but we do not know how he got it. The incubation period is 5-40 years. Farmed fish can be contaminated too. [http://www.naturalnews.com/026886\_disease\_farmed\_fish\_mad\_cow.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/026886_disease_farmed_fish_mad_cow.html)
>
>
>
> I do not believe the OIE. No one wants to scare people about possible contamination.

I'm sorry for your loss. Was it Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease?

CJD is at times called a human form of mad cow disease even though classic CJD is not related to bovine spongiform encephalopathy, however, given that bovine spongiform encephalopathy is believed to be the cause of variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob (vCJD) disease in humans the two are often confused. The defective protein can be transmitted by contaminated harvested human brain products, Immunoglobulins (IVIG), corneal grafts, dural grafts or electrode implants (acquired or iatrogenic form: iCJD); it can be inherited (hereditary or familial form: fCJD); or it may appear for the first time in the patient (sporadic form: sCJD). In the hereditary form, a mutation occurs in the gene for PrP, PRNP. 10-15% of cases are inherited.

It is thought that humans can contract the disease by consuming material from animals infected with the bovine form of the disease. The only suspected cases to arise thus far have been vCJD, although there are fears—based on animal studies—that consuming beef or beef products containing prion particles can also cause the development of classic CJD. When BSE material infects humans, the resulting disease is known as (new) variant CJD (nvCJD).

The USDA has banned the use of feeds derived from animal bone and blood meal. Additionally, specified risk materials for BSE (i.e. brains and spinal cords) are removed from the carcass and never enter the food supply.

I don't know how rare CJD is, but I would be hesitant to link it to BSE. It can also manifest in dogs, cats and other domestic pets.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-25T18:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/post-18751>

By the way, I believe in questioning everything, and right now, we know the government is a talking mouthpiece. However, as a beef producer myself, I firmly believe our beef supply is safe, and fortunately for us -- we have choices of how we prefer our beef produced to pick from at the meat counter.

You can debate the pros and cons of the quality of beef produced in many ways -- organic, natural, grass-fed, grain-fed, pastured-raised, etc., but never doubt the SAFETY of our food supply.

The biggest concern I have is more and more beef producers are being pushed out of business here in the U.S. The input costs are escalating, and the regulations imposed on us are extreme. As a result, we will import more of our beef, where we have no clue how it is raised or what safety rules must be followed from pasture-to-plate.

At the end of the day, I think it's imperative for everyone to support local farmers. Know the rancher, know the butcher. Make a connection. Here in SD, my husband and I proudly raise our own beef with my family, and his family continues the tradition of a custom, traveling butcher shop in MN. Support small, local, family-run cattle ranches. And, feel good about serving beef to your family.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-25T23:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beef-is-safe.1123/post-18853>

> **Jack Kruse;18846 wrote:** Do youhave a commercial site or are you a wholesaler?

We are a wholesaler, selling halves and quarters to friends, family and neighbors. I've had some inquiries on the forums for my beef from various parts of the country -- but the shipping costs get extremely cost prohibitive.

My husband and I both come from cattle backgrounds -- but we met while working at the meat shop at college. We both love the retail side of things, and my husband prides himself on being able to provide meat for our family -- from the beginning stage with new babies in the spring to the end stage of processing. He enjoys cutting specialty cuts like the flat iron steak, for example. Meanwhile, I love having my deep freezer full of pork, lamb, beef, deer, fish, goose, duck, rabbit, pheasant etc. It sure makes keto-paleo easy!

# Thread: lactic acid and melanin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lactic-acid-and-melanin.1127/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-25T17:27:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lactic-acid-and-melanin.1127/post-18706>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14756523](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14756523)

The inhibitory effect of glycolic acid and lactic acid on melanin synthesis in melanoma cells.

Usuki A, Ohashi A, Sato H, Ochiai Y, Ichihashi M, Funasaka Y.

Source

Division of Dermatology, Department of Clinical and Molecular Medicine, Kobe University Graduate School of Medicine, Kobe, Japan.

Abstract

Alpha-hydroxy acids (AHAs) such as glycolic acid (GA) and lactic acid (LA) have been reported to be effective in treating pigmentary lesions such as melasma, solar lentigines, and postinflammatory hyperpigmentation. The mechanism of this effect might be due to epidermal remodeling and accelerated desquamation, which would result in quick pigment dispersion. However, the direct effect of AHAs on melanin synthesis has not yet been well studied. To elucidate such a direct effect of AHAs on melanogenesis, we performed melanin assays, growth curve determinations, Northern and Western blotting for melanogenic proteins \[tyrosinase, tyrosinase related protein (TRP)-1 and TRP-2\], and tyrosinase and, 4-dihydroxyphenylalaninechrome tautomerase enzyme activity assays using mouse B16 and human melanoma cells. GA or LA (at doses of 300 or 500 microg/ml) inhibited melanin formation in similar dose-dependent manner, without affecting cell growth. Although the mRNA and protein expression or molecular size of tyrosinase, TRP-1 and TRP-2 were not affected, tyrosinase activity was inhibited. **To see whether GA and/or LA directly inhibit tyrosinase catalytic function, the effect of GA and LA on human tyrosinase purified from the melanosome-rich large granule fraction of human melanoma cells was performed. GA or LA were shown to inhibit tyrosinase enzyme activity directly, but this effect was not due to the acidity of GA or LA, because adjusting the pH to 5.6 (the pH of GA and LA at concentrations of 2500 microg/ml), did not affect tyrosinase activity. Taken together, these results show that GA and LA suppress melanin formation by directly inhibiting tyrosinase activity, an effect independent of their acidic nature. GA and LA might work on pigmentary lesions not only by accelerating the turnover of the epidermis but also by directly inhibiting melanin formation in melanocytes.**

interesting link [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skin\_whitening](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skin_whitening)

### Charmane — 2021-03-29T06:51:35+0300

That last one is a heckuva heady article there! Can't really understand... not yet anyway.

\*Is it that, if our fat-stores are inadequately "constructed," presumably because opsin3 has been messed up with artificial light (if the light surrounding us were "right," our fat-stores would be far more useful, perhaps even optimally so), cold-exposure can cause something like "pigment anomalies?"

If "yes," then that simply means we've got to get our whole selves into natural light as much as possible so that cold-exposure is not a problem? Given that I've probably still got a long way to go in terms of bettering my health, more new pigmented marks may show up for me as the years go forth... :/

I seem to have gotten my very first "liver spot" (I'm 52). It's on the inside of my right ankle. Just a faded brown spot; the skin as smooth as ever so it's not noticeable at all by touch. I'm wondering if it had anything to do with falling on the ice last month and being stuck indoors as the temperatures here went as low as 11 below zero. Every morning I opened my backdoor and took in the light for as long as I could stand both cold and pain... but that's all I could do. Otherwise, I was on the couch, resting, and getting on the laptop to challenge local stores to deliver groceries to my house despite all the ice. This spot seems to have shown up as soon as the weather got nice and I wasn't wearing 2 to 3 layers of pants anymore. I know the spot was not there on Wednesday, when I took a long magnesium salt bath and had my legs up on the wall in front of me. I wore a skirt and sandals to work on Friday, and didn't notice it then, either, but I got ready for work in a hurry. Saturday, I also wore a skirt/sandals and happened to glance down and see the spot, hoped it would wipe away, but it did not. Not thrilled, but at least it's not on my face or hands, and I think it's likely harmless enough.

I was fascinated with the fact that opsin3 is all over the surface of the skin and deep within the brain. Since cancerous brain tissue was removed from my right frontal lobe, the medical records say the tissue was brown in color. I'm now wondering about the pigment being "generated" within the tissue, if that makes sense. Could my brain possibly have "tried" to guard itself against the "wrong" light/frequencies coming into it? Oh my poor brain... I had no idea of how much damage I was risking... and taking...

### Jack Kruse — 2021-03-29T18:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lactic-acid-and-melanin.1127/post-296717>

WST-1 Cell Proliferation Assay is a sensitive and accurate assay for cell proliferation and cytotoxicity LDH cell membrane damage. The assay is highly convenient as it is performed in a single cell tissue culture well and requires no washing, harvesting, or solubilization of cells.

Adherent or suspension cells are cultured in a microplate and then incubated with WST-1 and the assay is monitored with a spectrophotometer. The assay principle is based upon the reduction of the tetrazolium salt WST-1 to formazan by cellular dehydrogenases. The generation of the dark yellow colored formazan is measured at 420-480nm (optimal at 440nm) and is directly correlated to cell number.
