# Thread: Anti-mitochondrial antibody test
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-mitochondrial-antibody-test.1216/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-01T00:05:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-mitochondrial-antibody-test.1216/post-20207>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-mitochondrial\_antibody](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-mitochondrial_antibody)

Anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are autoantibodies, consisting of immunoglobulins formed against mitochondria,\[1\] primarily mitochondria in cells of the liver. The presence of AMAs in the blood or serum of a person is indicative of several autoimmune diseases such as primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC) (a scarring of liver tissue, confined primarily to the bile duct drainage system of the liver). It is present in about 95% of cases.\[2\]

**Primary biliary cirrhosis is seen primarily in middle-aged women, and in those afflicted with other autoimmune diseases. PBC is an autoimmune disorder, a condition in which the human body's immune defense system mistakenly attacks the body's own cells, or in this case parts of the cells.**

Cause of AMAs is postulated that xenobiotic-induced and/or oxidative modification of mitochondrial autoantigens is a critical step leading to loss of tolerance. In acute liver failure AMA are found against all major liver antigens.\[3\]

Pyruvate dehydrogenase, E2 subunits

2-oxo-glutarate dehydrogenase

Branched chain 2-oxo-acid dehydrogenase

Anti-cardiolipin antibodies are another type of AMA, cardiolipin is found on the inner mitochondrial membrane.

### nonchalant — 2012-05-02T05:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-mitochondrial-antibody-test.1216/post-20613>

Antibodies against mitochondria?? Sounds like just about the worst thing you can have.

My mother in law has this after a kidney transplant. Spoke to her liver dr who has no clue what caused this besides “medication from her kidney transplant”…

Would deuterium depleted water help? Niacin has shown promise in treating liver fibrosis, particularly in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), by reversing hepatic steatosis and inflammation.

She is scared of the sun as she has skin cancer from her immune system being shut down and just had to get some skin growth removed and these were “fixed” by taking some skin grafts from her leg… so she is not looking forward to do this…

Her cataract also has become a major problem…

### Mat.I — 2026-04-17T13:52:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-mitochondrial-antibody-test.1216/post-371328>

> [Suigeneris said:](/goto/post?id=371318)
>
> My mother in law has this after a kidney transplant. Spoke to her liver dr who has no clue what caused this besides “medication from her kidney transplant”…

> Would deuterium depleted water help? Niacin has shown promise in treating liver fibrosis, particularly in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), by reversing hepatic steatosis and inflammation.

A blocked exhaust system caused all of these issues aka known as a plumbing issue...you need to figure out how to open the drain... DDW seems logical, morning + arvo sun, lots of grounding (cold face dunks while grounding), chew mastic gum, experiment with a small amount of bicarb first thing in morning (maybe 1/8 to 1/4 tsp in water) lower dose due to the recent transplant not close to any meals though.. maybe 40 mins to 1 hr away..

I would not hit the ddw to hard and fast and start at a higher ppm (say 105 to 125ppm) as you do not want to flood the system (you want to lower the back pressure slowly)..remember she has a new filtration system installed (dont clog it).. the transplant meds would be slowing the spin state also..

[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) - may have some ideas

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-17T16:25:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-mitochondrial-antibody-test.1216/post-371336>

Kidney is innervated by the Vagus nerve.

This new blog was inspired by patients with seizures, patients with atraumatic spleen ruptures, kidney disease, and Tourette's symdrome. An ecclectic mix to be sure. They were all connected to a Y-axis I never appreciated in my training. Now, in my life as a decentrlaized neurosurgeon, it is something I use everyday in treatment plans for my patients.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-155380212](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-155380212)

Without their personal storms I could have never come on this information in my career as a centralized neurosurgeon. Their personal storms taught me the error of my education and their personal storms were best medicine I ever received in my career.

Once the personal illness storm is over, you won’t remember how you made it through, how you managed to survive. You won’t even be sure, whether the storm is really over. But one thing is certain.

When you come out of the storm, you won’t be the same person who walked in. This is true for the patient and boy was it true for me. That’s what this storm’s all about. A message for the ages.

### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-01T03:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-the-ozarks.1222/post-20256>

Hello Everyone!

I've been lurking here and on MDA for a while, but I think it is time to join and see about getting a bit of help and maybe being of some help to others. I've been a bit of a physiological puzzle to myself for years, and I am impressed by the number of folks here who have had similar experiences. It is nice to find a group that is so willing to experiment to find the best possible health for ourselves and families.

I'll post some labs, ask some questions, all that good stuff soon. I'm thinking of doing a journal, too, as I've had all this health stuff on my mind forever, and doing a bit of writing, to clear out the old brain, might be good. And it may even be of benefit to someone else down the road.

Best to all of you. Thanks in advance. And especially thanks to Dr. Kruse. Your writings and ideas have been excellent thus far.

Tom

# Thread: Kids  Teeth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 69
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T04:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20269>

This was in 'Ask Jack' and so got answered and closed. But I have a more thorough answer, lol, and thought I'd share it because you know you're all entitled to my opinion.

> Just wondering what your feeling is about dental sealants for children. Is there anything harmful in the sealant or in the preparation of the tooth? What about filling small cavities in baby teeth? Prudent or not?

Sealants

\- usually contain BPA and/or other leaching plastics

\- usually fall out long before they're "supposed" to

\- often contain time-released fluoride (yeah, my kids' hygienist let this one out of the bag when I was asking about BPA)

\- often trap food particles and/or bacteria underneath, because the seal is not perfect

\- don't address the root cause of cavities AT ALL

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T04:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20270>

If you want to know about teeth, you MUST familiarize yourself with Weston Price. He was the lead researcher of what became known as the ADA, and wanted to know why kids' teeth were getting so crooked/misaligned, and adults & kids' teeth were getting so many cavities. He traveled the world in the 1930s - after photography became common, but before nearly every last tribe in the world started eating modern foods - and documented many groups which were still living their native lives and eating their native foods.

He found that **_so long as they ate only processed foods, they had almost no cavities - and also, no cancer, depression, obesity, diabetes, sociopaths, mental retardation, birth defects, or tuberculosis (the contagious killer of the day). He found that the closer they lived to the trading post (and hence, the more they traded for industrial food), the more they had these diseases of civilization, even with the same lifestyles.

He found that they all had certain things in common, no matter whether they lived in the Alps and ate sour rye, spring-fresh cheese, & goat; lived in Africa & ate nothing but meat, blood, & milk; lived in the South Pacific & ate fish, fruit, & insects; or lived in the Arctic and ate primarily seal blubber & whale oil - **they all had ten times the fat-soluble vitamins (that means animal fat) of the typical American **of the 1930s** (think how much more Americans ate then than now), all used organ meats, and most used broth, ferments, & (something else I just forgot). They also all spent at least six months before an expected pregnancy eating extra amounts of super foods like whole shellfish, liver, etc.**

Long story short, he did testing and found that eating fat-soluble vitamins (and enough magnesium) is CRUCIAL for the body knowing what to do with all the Calcium we shovel down our gullets. A bit of butter oil and fermented cod liver oil daily is enough to save teeth, but the more you eat of the animal sources of these vitamins, and especially K2, the better your teeth (and all other bones! think about scoliosis and osteoporosis!) will be.

I have used these principles to stop ALL the cavities in my family. My children all got cavities starting at age 18 months, even though they had almost no sugar or juices in their diets, and meticulous ADA-style home hygiene & checkups. As Price's data predicted (he observed that when children are born close together and/or the mother does not make the extraordinary effort that traditional groups made, each child has worse overall health/physical stature than the previous), each child's decay progressed more rapidly and more severely than the previous, to the point that my 4th child had lost his front top four teeth down to the gumline, and ALL his molars were (and are to this day) deeply grooved.

When they were 8, 5, & 2, we switched from skim to whole raw milk, and got rid of dry cereal. Their cavities stopped IMMEDIATELY. Two of them had abscesses that had bothered them a little bit (not much), and those stopped hurting, forever, right then. Those abscesses stayed in their gums, safely and painlessly draining, for the next few years till the affected teeth fell out._ All their baby teeth were replaced by beautiful, strong adult teeth. There have been no cavities in these once cavity-ridden children in five years.** (This despite removing all fluoride from our toothpaste & water & check-ups, no more x-rays, and increasingly rare toothpaste. I almost never brush my teeth anymore, NO CAVITIES, and NO halitosis - the latter stopped when I stopped the sugar fest.)

The fourth child wasn't quite as fortunate - he was still nursing from a sugar-addicted mother. I was trying to eat nutrient-dense, but too often binged and then skipped meals to make up for the calorie intake. When I realized that \*my\* diet was rapidly eroding what little of his teeth were left, I finally decided to stop the meal-skipping ... and the increased nutrient intake starting eroding the bingeing, surprise! The result of dramatically less sugar and thus, dramatically more nutrients, was that his teeth stabilized, and as he weaned, **he has, despite a disgusting mouth full of half-eroded or fully-eroded teeth, not had a single new or progressed cavity in almost three years.**

By the way, if you dig, you can find an interesting article from a mainstream British publication testifying that filling baby teeth does nothing to prevent pain or worsening infection or permanent damage, and says that dentists & parents should really take this into account when dealing with them. Basically, unless the tooth is causing pain that won't go away, fillings do nothing. Caps are purely cosmetic. Etc. (It was from July of 2008 or 2009, IIRC.) (Even if the tooth is causing pain, you can solve that with vitamin C megadosing, good info on how to know when things are going too far (extremely rare if you know how to megadose right), solid diet, & nerves of steel.)

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T04:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20272>

BTW, I'm sure you're wondering about #5, lol. I quit near-daily bingeing 3 months before her conception. (It took another few years to get every last binge out, as you well know.) So by the time she hit 18mo, she had a lot of good nutrition coming from breastmilk. We did see a tiny line form on one of the susceptible teeth (which now makes sense, as I found I have severe fat malabsorption from wheat damage to my gut villi, and so I must always be right on the edge of "not enough A/D/K2) after she decided she didn't like the variety of fermented cod liver oil we were using, and I promptly ordered a ton of the chocolate BO/fCLO combo gel she loves and BAM the line stopped.

We ran out of it and she started using the regular stuff last month. And then stopped. And the lines progressed across the top front four while the most susceptible (always seems to be the one on her right) started eroding. Got more BO/fCLO. All stopped again.

She is over 24mo now, ,and I expect the danger zone to be past soon. Her teeth are better than #4 or #3 or #2 by far, and maybe a little worse than #1 child's were at this age, so we have reversed the "more children = more degradation" trend in the cavity arena by superb nutrition. (Not soon enough to save her alignment, I'm afraid. She has the worst recessed chin & narrowest arch of all.   I would have had to change my diet a lot sooner to spare her that.)

### ColdBren — 2012-05-01T07:28:00+0300

Very interesting stuff. I think I will go ahead with the sealants for my 7 year old, but the dentist also wants to fill a "very small" cavity on her baby tooth. This is her first cavity, and I am leaning towards leaving it alone....maybe even trying to remineralize it with calcium and magnesium? You want to make the right decisions for your babies when it comes to the dentist, its such a traumatic experience in the first place, I want to avoid anything unnecessary, or worse, harmful. Everything I knew about cavities is wrong and I am amazed at all this new info.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T16:31:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20373>

> **Jack Kruse;20379 wrote: not all sealants have BPA.......just sayin
>
>
> Hence the word "usually."
>
>
>
>
> > **MJ\*;20390 wrote:** I'll have to ask what's in the sealants my kids have, but I have to say they are 17 and 15 years old and neither one has ever had a cavity. My son said to me on their last visit a few months ago, "Mom I don't really even know what a dentist does!" I was so happy to hear that LOL! I had tons of cavities as a kid. I've never let them have any type of sucking candy - that's the worst for cavities, but I do think the sealants have done their job.
>
>
>
> Sealants are only on the molars. They do sometimes cause cavities, and they do sometimes (maybe - if cavities are \*ever\* caused by food particles, and I'm just dubious about that one - remember, I almost never brush my teeth unless there are obvious food particles, and never ever ever floss, and have no cavities) prevent them.
>
>
>
> I had sealants as a child, and have no idea if I still have them or not. I did have them done on my oldest, and a year later, two were already missing. I did not have them re-done, and she has no problems with those teeth.
>
>
>
> Keep in mind that all three of my oldest had nonstop cavities from the time they were 18 months till we changed their diets - that's 6+ years for my oldest - and when the diet changed, the cavities stopped in all of them, with no cavities in any child or adult in our house in the five years since then. Not one. No progression of the old, numerous "cavities" (in many cases, there wasn't enough of a tooth let to call it a cavity). Complete stability. No pain.
>
>
>
> Cavities are NOT genetic, so yours as a child has little to do with your children's likelihood of getting them. As with any "disease of modern civilization," the tendency to get dental caries \*if\* your nutritional stores are inadequate can be passed down, but it is 100% preventable. How is your children's diet compared to yours as a child? Could it be their genetic tendencies here come from their father? I would not be so hasty to assume the sealants are responsible.
>
>
>
> My children's caries had NO genetic predisposition. Not a single person in either side of the family going back several generations had cavities as early or as often or as severe as they did. None.
>
> ColdBren;20391 wrote:** Very interesting stuff. I think I will go ahead with the sealants for my 7 year old, but the dentist also wants to fill a "very small" cavity on her baby tooth. This is her first cavity, and I am leaning towards leaving it alone....maybe even trying to remineralize it with calcium and magnesium? You want to make the right decisions for your babies when it comes to the dentist, its such a traumatic experience in the first place, I want to avoid anything unnecessary, or worse, harmful. Everything I knew about cavities is wrong and I am amazed at all this new info.

Don't be afraid to do the research on sealants first. There is NO rush. Does he even have permanent molars yet? If the dentist is pushing sealants for baby teeth, RUN!!!!

I wouldn't touch the cavity, ESPECIALLY if there is no pain. Not a chance. I'd definitely use it as a warning that more are to come, and something needs to change. And that's not likely the hygiene. (Keep in mind that another dentist, equally as modern & conventional, may say there is no cavity at all. Dentists disagree with each other a LOT.) You want to put a stop to this before it starts hitting the permanent teeth.

Dentistry is fear-based, just like the rest of modern medicine. Do your research on sealants first. Ask yourself about human history, and what makes sense. Of course, your child's diet matters tremendously, but even if it's not perfect (whose is?), fCLO & pastured dairy fat & pastured egg yolks &/or grassfed liver &/or wild-caught fish roe can make up for a LOT.

You have to have the fat soluble vitamins to use the Ca & Mg, or they will be useless.

I am kind of blessed in a way to have gone through all this, because had it not been for having to take my oldest children repeatedly to the dentist for extremely traumatizing procedures multiple times before they were three years old, I don't know if I ever would have questioned anything. And they would have never learned, and would have gone down the road of having lost most of their teeth by 60 or 70 like today's adults have (they call them "root canals" and "bridges" and "crowns" but if it weren't for modern prosthetic dentistry, they would have big open spaces where those teeth used to be). Heck, my grandmother had a full set of dentures at 38, in the 1970s!

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T17:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20396>

Spot on mama grok. I unfortunately was born with a small jaw but my

Mother breastfed all of us for over a year, and because my parents were big foodies/gourmets we always had beautiful raw artisan cheeses around the house, organic meats, eggs, whole

Milk, organ meats and my sisters and I all would fight over who got the organs from the roasted chicken. (my dad would fry the giblets and l livers up in butter for us and we'd

Share.) I ate a TON of crawfish and oysters as a toddler living in New Orleans (yes... Raw and I sucked the heads of the crawfish.). Anyway none of my 2 siblings and I have ever had a cavity and we ate lots of "whole grains." I had a couple funky areas of decay after 6 months of nursing but they've since disappeared. I did get fat after I moved from my parents house but it was because I bought cheap food.

This is n=1 but I think raw dairy and shellfish are where it's at with cavity prevention. I had horrid oral hygiene habits as a kid... Seriously. Maybe my

Sealants helped a bit but I had plenty of friends with sealants who got cavities and I also had bulimia as a teenager so I should've had all sorts of problems with my teeth.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T17:47:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20414>

> **MJ\*;20477 wrote:**
>
> I can't handle organ meats,

I can't, either. I have found the "perfect" way to cook liver & onions that liver lovers think is gourmet, but I still can't. I hold my breath. Every day I eat raw liver without tasting it. I take cod liver oil without tasting it. I eat kidneys chopped in teeny tiny bits 1:4 with round steak and cook it till it's all blended together and can't be tasted. I mix ground liver 1:4 with ground beef and blend it well so it can't be tasted. I order US Wellness Meats liverwurst, and all kinds of traditional organ sausage products from local farmers, like braunschweiger and a bunch of other names I can't remember. I make chicken livers into pate and still hold my breath b/c I can't stand it. I eat roe while holding my breath. I eat oysters & mussels & whole sardines while holding my breath. I eat stuff like tongue & heart fine, so long as it's cooked well (or jerkified in the latter case), b/c they have no particular flavor, and blend in easily with other slow-roasted beef.

My health sucks too much to avoid offal. And so far as I can tell (haven't heard anything to the contrary despite asking many times), K2 comes in two forms commercially: MK4 & MK7.. MK7 is from plants and doesn't have the bone (teeth) benefits. MK4 is only available as a synthetic. I don't do synthetic, especially not for fat-soluble vitamins; I just don't. So it's tasteless offal-swallowing for me!

>
>
> you've got to try US Wellness Meats LIVERWURST - To DIE FOR! I open a package and its gone w/in 3days btwn me and the kids! Ok I will look into it...my mother loved liverwurst, she was the only one who would eat it...so stanky smelling...but I will look for it and think about it...
>
>
>
> MamaGrok;20487 wrote:** I can't, either. I have found the "perfect" way to cook liver & onions that liver lovers think is gourmet, but I still can't. I hold my breath. Every day I eat raw liver without tasting it. I take cod liver oil without tasting it. I eat kidneys chopped in teeny tiny bits 1:4 with round steak and cook it till it's all blended together and can't be tasted. I mix ground liver 1:4 with ground beef and blend it well so it can't be tasted. I order US Wellness Meats liverwurst, and all kinds of traditional organ sausage products from local farmers, like braunschweiger and a bunch of other names I can't remember. I make chicken livers into pate and still hold my breath b/c I can't stand it. I eat roe while holding my breath. I eat oysters & mussels & whole sardines while holding my breath. I eat stuff like tongue & heart fine, so long as it's cooked well (or jerkified in the latter case), b/c they have no particular flavor, and blend in easily with other slow-roasted beef.
>
>
>
> My health sucks too much to avoid offal. And so far as I can tell (haven't heard anything to the contrary despite asking many times), K2 comes in two forms commercially: MK4 & MK7.. MK7 is from plants and doesn't have the bone (teeth) benefits. MK4 is only available as a synthetic. I don't do synthetic, especially not for fat-soluble vitamins; I just don't. So it's tasteless offal-swallowing for me!

_What a woman!!!_ I bow down to you, both of you really...I feel like the actress in Indiana Jones when they were eating the monkey brains, it makes me want to pass out LOL!!! So MK4 is only found in these meats? I am getting my testing in a month, there's so much I don't understand yet...I have been so healthy my whole life, never, ever sick, not even the flu in years...but if my tests say I need this, I'll revisit the thought....I love how you hide it in your other foods that's a great way to do it...

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T19:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20455>

MK4 is found primarily in \*pastured\* liver, egg yolks, & butter; fermented cod liver oil, wild fish roe, that's about it, I think.

Organ meat isn't primarily for immunity. It's for bones, eyes, every organ, proper detoxification of crap, etc., etc., etc. Look up any vitamin you can think of, and if you find a chart that bothers to list animal sources, liver will be above everything, including every vegetable. It dwarfs plants for vitamin C, A, K2, all the B's, and many minerals. Liver is a true superfood.

I've eaten liver, kidney, marrow, thyroid, thymus, eye, intestines, tongue, tripe (what is that?), whole shellfish (which includes every organ), gizzard, jowl, feet, tail, whatever I can find or beg for. I have ear & testicles in my freezer but haven't found the recipe/courage for them yet.

I've also taken Dr. Ron's Organ Delight, a tasteless capsule mix of many organs.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T19:20:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20461>

More k1 than k2 in liver. In dissection studies done on humans and rats (which is all I could find in my scholarly searches) the most k2 was found in pancreas, kidney, brain. They didn't test the bone marrow unfortunately, but that is another suspected source of k2. Another suspected source is the coral in crabs, crawfish, and lobster (the funky yellow gunk in the head cavity.)

### LinD — 2012-05-03T06:31:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20909>

MamaGrok,

I find it interesting that a bump on my palate finally went away a couple weeks ago that appeared in December. I saw my DDS in January. Had an xray, but not much to see. He prescribed antibiotics, but it didn't help. So went back a couple weeks later. Another xray seemed to show some darkness in the root area or something. He said it was an abscess and I needed a root canal. I had no pain, by the way. Scheduled the appointment, but had to postpone it due to DH's L4L5 surgery (where he had massive pain!).

Long story short, it was 99.9% gone two weeks ago. But, it started coming back after about a week. What the heck? Well, I realized I slowed down on the Vitamin C when I noticed the bump was almost gone--I was taking probably 3000 or more each day in hopes it would help the abscess go away.

When I realized the possible Vitamin C connection, I started up again with 3000-4000/day.... Guess what? It's going back down again. I expect it will be nearly gone over the next several days.

How about that?

Sprint Samsung Epic

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-03T15:09:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20936>

Woohoo! I have had many close calls with abscesses in my boys when we first decided to hit the diet and take the wait-and-see approach with the abscesses.

One time, my 3rd child had pain start (probably after a weekend at grandma's), and then pink started to spread up his cheek. Well, that was my "go straight to the pediatrician and get a shot of antibiotic" signal, but I decided to hit the C HARD. Fortunately, he was old enough to be cooperative. I did drive him to the ped, she prescribed antibx, and we went to the pharmacy to fill them.

Meanwhile, the pink had spread halfway up his cheek and started to harden a bit by time we got to the ped. She drew a line around it with pen. But by the time we got home with the drugs (taking C all along, for a total of six hours and who knows how many tens of thousands of mg), the pink & hardness had started to reverse! I kept up the C and the drugs were never used. It does take it a few hours to kick in hard enough sometimes.

Not everyone is willing to (or informed enough to) go that far, as Rami Nagel says in Cure Tooth Decay. His daughter also had teeth worn to the gumline before he figured out how to stop it. She woke up pink-faced one morning and he treated it successfully, but you do have to know what to do at each step, what your "it's too far for me" step is, etc. I had thought that any pink on th eface at all was too far gone for me, which is why I went to the doc & got the meds, but ... the pink reversed! What can I say?! (I did check it every few hours in the night to be sure it didn't start up again and we kept at the C to bowel tolerance for at least another day or two.)

I've also used C to reverse an abscessed bug bite on that son's knee, before I could get him home with the antibiotics.

You could also try topical and internal garlic and/or coconut oil. (I used thin slices of garlic, replaced once or twice a day, to get rid of the painful infection in a wart I mindlessly picked.)

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-03T16:07:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-20955>

I have the book. Rami has found, from Price's research, exactly the same conclusions as I have. Foods rich in K2, D, Magnesium, etc., are _essential_ for dental health. C is the medication if the foods aren't eaten in enough quantity to prevent infection. So C is not your first line of defense.

I also strongly prefer food forms of the nutrients to supplements, because I have yet to find K2 that is not synthetic.

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-10T21:50:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-22976>

My kids have been on a strict paleo diet for several years, raw milk, cod liver oil, vit. D, tons of pasture butter, pastured eggs etc. They hadn't had any cavities until ages 5 and 7, when all of a sudden they had a ton. I let them go, hoping that pushing bone broth and more fat vitamins would help, but it was worse when we went back again, including some in their permanent molars. I even had them brushing, flossing and waterpicking after every meal, swishing with a minerals mixed in water and no snacking. I also had removed all fruit. Finally I had their teeth filled because I didn't know what else to do, they were permanent teeth, nutrition didn't seem to be working etc.

Then, I remembered that I had started chelating the kids for heavy metals. Part of the protocol was to take tons on minerals because it can depelte those. I couldn't get the kids to swallow pills, so I had hoped the bone broth, raw milk and some herbal mineral tea would be enough. Now I think that might not have been and could have been what weakened their teeth. I stopped the chelation for them and we haven't been back to the dentist yet, but I'm hoping that's what caused it.

I'm just sick about their teeth. I thought they would be immune to cavities with such an amazing diet, tons of teeth cleaning and a Mom who has never had a cavity (although Dad has lots). I even have a Paleo dentist who prescribes diet to his clients to heal their teeth who told me I should have them filled, because I had already done all I possibly could to heal them and it just didn't work (he doesn't see kids himself though).

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-11T00:02:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-23028>

So sorry to hear that difficulty, paleoMom! I know the pain we feel when this stuff happens to our kids. Sounds like you did the best you could at every point along the line, and I hope your plan works!

### MamaGrok — 2012-06-01T16:20:15+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-06-01T17:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-28864>

Classic Weston Price. He found that in families on traditional foods with the naturally built 2.5-3.5 year spacing, the youngest was as healthiest as the oldest. If either of those factors was not true, in general, each child was in worse health in some way (or many) than the previous.

In my kids, it's the teeth, and as you get to the fifth, the palate, as well. I think the spacing saved them from much worse.

There's a reason that every single traditional society across the world fed its soon-to-conceive young men and its soon-to-conceive, pregnant, or nursing young women superfoods, going to great lengths to obtain them. I like to think of those great lengths when I'm driving an hour and a half to pick up my grassfed cow or paying out the wazoo for fermented cod liver oil.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-30T18:52:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-teeth.1223/post-34617>

BPA is becoming a hot topic in the dental world. I'm a Dental Rep for the largest materials manufacturer in the world, and we came out with a letter to dentists last year stating that we do not use BPA in anything we manufacture. I cannot tell you how many times I've had to reprint this letter, as patients are asking. I even had one Pediatric Dentist call me for an emergency email--the patient (about 5 years old) was sedated, and the parent would not let her continue without seeing that no BPA was going into her child's mouth. Up to a couple of years ago, we still did have BPA, but we have changed those formulations or discontinued those items so we can say we are 100% "BPA Free".

I personally had my dentist replace my old fillings (not amalgam, but I'm sure full of BPA) with our materials. Of course, as the rep, I bring my own materials to my dentist and hand them to her as she's working...lol. I'm glad to see so many patients aware of BPA.

# Thread: phytic acid Tufts University collection of studies

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4

### chocolate — 2012-05-01T05:38:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-tufts-university-collection-of-studies.1225/post-20296>

[http://www.lahey.org/Departments\_and\_Locations/Departments/Dermatology/Ebsco\_Content/Shingles.aspx?chunkiid=21766](http://http://www.lahey.org/Departments_and_Locations/Departments/Dermatology/Ebsco_Content/Shingles.aspx?chunkiid=21766)

Supplement Forms/Alternate Names:

IP6; Inositol Hexaphosphate; Myoinositol; Phytic Acid; Vitamin B 8

Principal Proposed Uses

Depression ; Panic Disorder

Other Proposed Uses

Alzheimer's Disease ; Attention Deficit Disorder ; Bipolar Disorder ; Bulimia ; Cancer Treatment Support ; Cancer Prevention ; Diabetic Neuropathy ; Metabolic Syndrome ; Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder; Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS) ; Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS) ; Psoriasis Caused by Treatment With Lithium

Sources

Therapeutic Dosages

Therapeutic Uses

What Is the Scientific Evidence for Inositol?

Safety Issues

References

Inositol, unofficially referred to as "vitamin B 8," is present in all animal tissues, with the highest levels in the heart and brain. It is part of the membranes (outer coverings) of all cells, and plays a role in helping the liver process fats as well as contributing to the function of muscles and nerves. ....more on the link

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-01T05:43:43+0300

### chocolate — 2012-05-02T19:37:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-tufts-university-collection-of-studies.1225/post-20720>

What are the benefits of Prescription B vitamins?

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/438838-what-are-the-benefits-of-prescription-b-complex-vitamins/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/438838-what-are-the-benefits-of-prescription-b-complex-vitamins/)

B-complex vitamins refer to a variety of essential water-soluble vitamins that include thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folic acid and cobalamins. These vitamins perform different functions in the body, including energy production, facilitation of cell division and amino acid metabolism. B-complex vitamins are not manufactured by the body. Since each person's need for vitamins is different, it is best to have a doctor determine B-complex requirements

Read more: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/438838-what-are-the-benefits-of-prescription-b-complex-vitamins/#ixzz1tjNrhMWR](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/438838-what-are-the-benefits-of-prescription-b-complex-vitamins/#ixzz1tjNrhMWR)

### chocolate — 2012-05-04T19:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-tufts-university-collection-of-studies.1225/post-21357>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15850106](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15850106)

Growth and cell viability of estradiol and IP-6 treated Hep-2 laryngeal carcinoma cells.

Inositol 6-phosphate (IP-6) has demonstrated novel anti-cancer activity using several different tumor models. IP-6, a phytoestrogen, has estrogen receptor (ER) binding capabilities that are not known to cause cellular proliferation in hormone sensitive cells. It is hypothesized that IP6 can induce competitive inhibition with estrogen for estrogenic binding sites on cancer cells resulting in decreased proliferation. In this experiment, Hep-2 cells were treated with Estradiol and IP-6 in a dose dependant manner for 24, 48, and 72 hours. They were analyzed for changes in number, protein concentrations, damage, and morphology. There was an increase in cell proliferation in Estradiol treated Hep-2 cells. Cells treated with IP-6 showed no change in cell proliferation in the 24 or 48-hour groups, but there was a decrease in number with the 72-hour group, particularly with the 1mM dose. Both the Estradiol and IP-6 treatments caused no membrane oxidation and the level of protein synthesis stayed consistent. The morphology showed small round to cuboidal, single cells with scant, dense, basophilic cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei with smooth borders. **Some cells showed anucleation and cellular degeneration. Although IP-6 is a phytoestrogen, the results show that affinity for estrogen binding sites on Hep-2 cells is greatly decreased. However, with time given increased concentrations, IP-6 can cause a decrease in the cellular proliferation of Hep-2 cells without initiating cellular apoptosis.

**

# Thread: Isolated Vitamin d3 supplementation is useless?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T17:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-20401>

[http://www.mommypotamus.com/why-vitamin-d-supplements-cant-replace-sunshine/](http://http://www.mommypotamus.com/why-vitamin-d-supplements-cant-replace-sunshine/)

Has anyone heard this? I mean I try to get some sunlight whenever possible but living in new England, there's a large part of the year where lots of sun is rare. Meanwhile, I've been taking Cod liver oil and isolated d3 , (and, of course k2 and various minerals) to keep my D levels above 50 for most of the year. If this is true and the sulfate form of vitamin D (and other various compounds created by the sun in the skin) is the most important , does anyone have any idea how to mitigate this problem during the dark/rainy seasons? Are tanning beds a bad idea?

### nonchalant — 2012-05-01T18:50:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-20444>

Also, I don't know where I read it, but there are several phytonutrients that are created from the effect of sunlight on our skin, we are just familiar with vitamin D. The other ones are probably important.

Perhaps there is a compensating factor there? If you're cold-adapted, the lack of sun isn't as much of an issue? I think I remember Dr. K saying if you have enough Vitamin D stored up by Autumn, you're ok all winter. Or something like that...

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T20:08:00+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-01T20:11:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-20492>

When the skin makes Vitamin D3 naturally, usually 10-20,000 IU are made locally in the skin. The excess Vitamin D 3 is then broken down to its degradation products. These degradation products have been shown to inhibit the development of psoriasis in studies. These degradation products prevent the proliferation of the lower levels of the skin from reproducing at a faster rate than normal. This is the pathology found in psoriasis. The lower epidermis is known to grow 25-40 times faster and the skin gets a large red plaque on its surface as a result. This is why light therapy is still so effective in treating psoriasis today. Its kind of funny that dermatologists don’t look at the pathologic causes of this disease here. Here sun light is curative because it stimulates Vitamin D 3 production to make excess Vitamin D3 to make degradation products. Anyone with psoriasis should have their Vitamin D levels checked and optimized before they do anything else. Most have extremely low levels and they tend to be obese and have higher cancer rates across the board as well. Why? Low vitamin D levels is why!

Vitamin D levels above 50 ng/ml are associated with increased adiponectin levels. High adiponectin means you are less likely to be obese. So higher vitamin D levels can help us trim our fat stores. By decreasing fat levels it can help us avoid the initial steps needed to begin insulin resistance and eventually develop Type 2 Diabetes.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T20:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-20501>

Hmmmm at my last blood draw a few months ago my D levels were about 75. I am obese, though I am slowly but surely losing the weight (down 50 so far,) and I still have a little bit of psoriasis left on my knees, though the elbows have disappeared. I wonder if I would benefit from some tanning. I never wear sunscreen or wear hats but it's still pretty dark here even in the spring. Would a tanning salon provide similar benefits in your opinion?

### chocolate — 2012-05-10T15:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-22806>

[http://advancedmediterranean.com/2012/05/10/take-vitamin-d-with-largest-meal-to-increase-blood-level/](http://http://advancedmediterranean.com/2012/05/10/take-vitamin-d-with-largest-meal-to-increase-blood-level/)

Take Vitamin D With Largest Meal to Increase Blood Level

The Healthy Librarian at the Happy Healthy Long Life blog wrote about a small scientific study documenting an incredibly easy way to increase blood levels of vitamin D in people taking supplemental vitamin D:

Take the supplement with the largest meal of the day

Subjects of this research were taking vitamin D supplementsâ€”often a very high doseâ€”for medical reasons, yet blood levels remained unacceptably low. Blood levels of vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D) rose by 50% simply by taking the same dose with the largest daily meal.

Other people, including young healthy adults, may or may not respond the same way. Do you know?

As for me, Iâ€™ll be sure to take my vitamin D supplement with my largest meal.

These degradation products have been shown to inhibit the development of psoriasis in studies. These degradation products prevent the proliferation of the lower levels of the skin from reproducing at a faster rate than normal. This is the pathology found in psoriasis. The lower epidermis is known to grow 25-40 times faster and the skin gets a large red plaque on its surface as a result. This is why light therapy is still so effective in treating psoriasis today. Its kind of funny that dermatologists donâ€™t look at the pathologic causes of this disease here. Here sun light is curative because it stimulates Vitamin D 3 production to make excess Vitamin D3 to make degradation products. Anyone with psoriasis should have their Vitamin D levels checked and optimized before they do anything else. Most have extremely low levels and they tend to be obese and have higher cancer rates across the board as well. Why? Low vitamin D levels is why!
>
>
>
> Vitamin D levels above 50 ng/ml are associated with increased adiponectin levels. High adiponectin means you are less likely to be obese. So higher vitamin D levels can help us trim our fat stores. By decreasing fat levels it can help us avoid the initial steps needed to begin insulin resistance and eventually develop Type 2 Diabetes.

I am printing this out and taking it to my doctor next Friday. Mine was low the last time I had a blood test. I upped my dosage and have been taking it with k2. K2 had done wonders for me as far as not having high calcium in my blood anymore. Psoriasis is still present on left elbow and will see about taking much higher doses of D3

@Chocolate, eggs (sulpher) and d3. Sound good?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-11T14:53:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-23136>

totally useless......not all the time but it is better if balanced with A K2 and E all versions

### chocolate — 2012-05-14T17:38:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/isolated-vitamin-d3-supplementation-is-useless.1228/post-23908>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2077285/?tool=pmcentrez](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2077285/?tool=pmcentrez)

Sulforaphane mobilizes cellular defenses that protect skin against damage by UV radiation

UV radiation (UVR) is a complete carcinogen that elicits a constellation of pathological events, including direct DNA damage, generation of reactive oxidants that peroxidize lipids and damage other cellular components, initiation of inflammation, and suppression of the immune response. Recent dramatic increases in the incidence of nonmelanoma skin cancers are largely attributable to higher exposure of an aging population to UVR. Therefore, the development of cellular strategies for intrinsic protection of the skin against the deleterious effects of UVR is imperative. **Here we show that erythema resulting from UVR is a comprehensive and noninvasive biomarker for assessing UVR damage and can be precisely and easily quantified in human skin. Topical application of sulforaphane-rich extracts of 3-day-old broccoli sprouts up-regulated phase 2 enzymes in the mouse and human skin, protected against UVR-induced inflammation and edema in mice, and reduced susceptibility to erythema arising from narrow-band 311-nm UVR in humans. In six human subjects (three males and three females, 28–53 years of age), the mean reduction in erythema across six doses of UVR (300–800 mJ/cm2 in 100 mJ/cm2 increments) was 37.7% (range 8.37–78.1%; P = 0.025). This protection against a carcinogen in humans is catalytic and long lasting.**

# Thread: Zinc from sunscreen is absorbed through the skin: study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-01T17:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/post-20402>

[http://www.abc.net.au/pm/content/2010/s2830477.htm](http://http://www.abc.net.au/pm/content/2010/s2830477.htm)

MARK COLVIN: There have long been concerns that the zinc used in sunscreen could be absorbed through the skin and into the body. A series of studies have shown no evidence of that - until now.

Scientists at Sydney's Macquarie University have found that zinc does penetrate the body and that it stays around for extended periods of time although not in big enough amounts to be harmful.

The researchers included controversial zinc nano-particles in their tests, but it's not clear what role, if any, the nano-particles played in the absorption process.

They presented their findings this week at a conference in Sydney on nanotechnology.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T17:51:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/post-20417>

Easy solution is to not use sunscreen. However for spots like your ears or nose there ARE brands that don't use nano-particles. I think badger brand doesn't and California baby.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-01T18:30:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/post-20436>

More and more topical stuff for supplementation (mag oil, iodine, hormone creams, etc) and then people are surprised when they hear that they absorb the "other" stuff as well (your shampoo, your lotion, your make up). Scrutinize your toothpaste/mouthwash as well, sublingual absorption is excellent.

I'd bet that the zinc isn't the worst of what is absorbed from that sunscreen...

Dr K says that our gut is the point of interface between our bodies and the environment and therefore vulnerable to insult first... what about our skin.

### chocolate — 2012-05-01T18:43:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/post-20441>

Zinc is how virii link up somehow and has to do with altered genetics....and if you don't have zinc, your estrogen runs wild. Zinc is what switches it off. I think you need it closer to your eyes though, zinc and tyrosine are really important. I have to look it up again, so I don't sound like some lame assed Oracle of Delphi.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-from-sunscreen-is-absorbed-through-the-skin-study.1229/post-22262>

I use a zinc based sunscreen (Pratima Neem Vetiver SPF 30) and I think it actually helps keep my skin more clear _because_ of the absorption of the zinc (it's very clean- no titanium or chemicals- and it's light enough that your skin doesn't feel smothered or look white!)

The ingredients are: IONIZED WATER, NON-NANO MICRONIZED ZINC OXIDE 18.6%, ORGANIC EXTRACTS OF NEEM, LICORICE, IMPERATA CYLINDRICA EXTRACT, VITAMIN E, LECITHIN, ORGANIC ESSENTIAL OIL OF VETIVER, XANTHAN GUM.)

I mostly use it on my face, neck and chest to prevent photoaging/excessive freckling, but I sometimes use it on my body if I'm going to be out in the sun a long time (I have fair skin that doesn't get very tan).

# Thread: Cravings - good or bad?!?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-05-02T20:52:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20748>

Obviously some cravings are definitely going to be bad, i.e. sugar, crackers, etc, etc. Fortunately I haven't had cravings for sweets in a very long time. I do find myself with a super strong desire for red cabbage though. I suppose I should be happy my only craving is for a real food and one that has a nice punch of antioxidants at that, but why on earth is my body craving it so badly?!?!

I have literally eaten a head of cabbage in 2 days! Would you be concerned? Could this be a bad craving in some way? I'm assuming maybe there's something in the cabbage that my body needs, but then I wonder too with such a strong craving if it could be something bad. I ran out of it this morning and had to run to the health food store on my lunch break to get more. I'm salivating at the thought of it and am actually ticked because I won't be able to make it in time for my dinner tonight!

I bake it nice and slow in the oven loaded with coconut oil and a little sea salt and it honestly tastes like heaven to me : )

### ealachan — 2012-05-02T23:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20819>

I had the same thing a few weeks ago with shrimp. I craved it really strongly for about two weeks, actually. I ate probably close to a pound of shrimp in one sitting, then a week later did nearly the same thing, and haven't thought about it since.

I think that while some cravings can definitely stem from habit, addiction, etc...others are just your body's way of yelling at you that it needs something.

Just my two cents, anyway.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-03T03:59:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20883>

You might be dumping excess estrogen. Cruciferous can help to balance the E dump. I'm E dominate And pretty much the only veggies I like are kale, cabbage, collards, broccoli, burssel sprouts. This has been intuitive for me.

### Glamorama — 2012-05-03T04:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20892>

> **Shijin13;20957 wrote:** You might be dumping excess estrogen. Cruciferous can help to balance the E dump. I'm E dominate And pretty much the only veggies I like are kale, cabbage, collards, broccoli, burssel sprouts. This has been intuitive for me.

Same, expect I only prefer bruseel sprouts, broccoli, and cabbage. Not craving them, but its the only veggies I eat atm.

### BJK77 — 2012-05-03T16:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20971>

I didn't realize cruciferous veggies help with E dumping. That makes perfect sense. My body totally craves brussel sprouts, broccoli and cauliflower too, but I haven't been able to get any fresh, local ones yet. I've been fortunate enough to find cabbage and kale, so that's what I've been going with. It's just odd that I only want the red cabbage, but I'm guessing that may have something to do with the antioxidant/polyphenol content.

Interesting . . . I swear I'm learning something new every day around here!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-03T16:38:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20974>

I LOVE cruciferous veggies, too, but they are bad for your thyroid...so while I want to eat them all the time, I have to wonder how much they hurt me in the long-run. (I have hashis).

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-05-03T17:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cravings-good-or-bad.1248/post-20998>

once the cruciferous are cooked they are less detrimental for the thyroid and if you are really concerned you could simply adjust your thyroid meds to compensate.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-03T17:24:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-20988>

ok ladies

what brand of DIM do you use... think I need to start taking this... I'm having serious detox from the CT... the craziness abounds!

### ealachan — 2012-05-03T17:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-20990>

I just bought the kind on the "Jack Recommends" page...literally, yesterday afternoon via Amazon. Never taken it before, so will be curious to see how it goes.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-03T17:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-20997>

some of the reviews mentioned migraine when they took it (which for me I'm trying to get rid of them w/the BHRT!) ... I've also seen great reviews on nature made... it also has less fillers... it cost less to... I may just buy that one...

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-05-03T17:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21000>

I used one by Douglas Labs that I got at iherb on sale, felt it worked really well but then they went and discontinued it after only having it for 2 months

[http://www.iherb.com/Douglas-Laboratories-DIM-with-Curcumin-Green-Tea-and-Wasabia-60-Veggie-Caps-Discontinued-Item/41782](http://http://www.iherb.com/Douglas-Laboratories-DIM-with-Curcumin-Green-Tea-and-Wasabia-60-Veggie-Caps-Discontinued-Item/41782)

using Source Naturals right now and it seems okay but will order the other from Amazon when this is done.

### ealachan — 2012-05-03T17:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21002>

> **Shijin13;21072 wrote:** some of the reviews mentioned migraine when they took it (which for me I'm trying to get rid of them w/the BHRT!) ... I've also seen great reviews on nature made... it also has less fillers... it cost less to... I may just buy that one...

Eeek! I'm not prone to migraines, so hopefully that stuff won't give me one. Guess that's what I get for not reading the reviews first. :

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-03T18:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21021>

I have been using Natures Way DIM Plus for over a month and like it. I can really tell the estrogen is not as high in my body and detox symps seem to have lessened to almost none.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-03T19:06:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21035>

I use the pure powder from beyondacentury.... cheap and no fillers

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-05-04T00:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21135>

> **Shijin13;21063 wrote:** ok ladies
>
>
>
> what brand of DIM do you use... think I need to start taking this... I'm having serious detox from the CT... the craziness abounds!

I think the DIM from Jack Recommends is the best b/c it has phospholipids, vit. E etc. for better absorption. For me though in Canada, I just ordered some 'Young Again' which actually ships, has Paypal option etc. The Young Again capsules have as other ingredients in the supplement: gelatin, rice flour and magnesium stearate. I hope the rice flour is not a problem...and cancel out the DIM. I think I will not worry about the 'absorption' as I will make sure I take it with breakfast which has plenty of fat anyway.

Hope this helps.

### IceBarbie — 2012-05-04T03:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21198>

Here are the ingredients in the Olympia Lab brand that I have been using for about 4 months now: (hubby is on it too for his Man Boobs or MOOBS)

Microcrystalline cellulose (plant fiber), rice flour, ascorbyl palmitate and silica.

All Olympian Labs' products are free from most allergens such as corn, yeast, barley, gluten, wheat, soy, lactose, all milk products, fish, egg products, as well as added flavorings, sugars, sweeteners, salt, preservatives, salicylates, and artificial colors and coatings.

[http://www.iherb.com/Olympian-Labs-Inc-DIM-60-Capsules/15231](http://http://www.iherb.com/Olympian-Labs-Inc-DIM-60-Capsules/15231)

I take 400 mg's per day. The other "Extra Strength" version by Olympia Labs contains 150 mg's in each capsule. I don't think it's as good of a deal as the one on this link.

$5 off your first order with code: OYE996

### vkiernan — 2012-05-04T03:59:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21200>

> **Patty Cakes;21096 wrote:** I have been using Natures Way DIM Plus for over a month and like it. I can really tell the estrogen is not as high in my body and detox symps seem to have lessened to almost none.

I am using the same thing for the past couple of months. I think natural girl uses it too and that is why I tried it. I was on something else before. I went thru serious detox with my last cycle and don't know if this played a part or it was all CT as I had been on it about 1 month by then.

Just curious how you know you have less estrogen? I think I don't due to no breast tenderness.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-04T04:05:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21203>

> **ealachan;21065 wrote:** I just bought the kind on the "Jack Recommends" page...literally, yesterday afternoon via Amazon. Never taken it before, so will be curious to see how it goes.

me too!!!!!! my E2 came back at 40......so it works.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-05-04T04:31:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dim-what-brand-do-you-use.1263/post-21216>

Well I'm 48 yr old, so I just want to caution you ladies that your estradiol levels can bottom out with Dim, so monitor your levels! My E2 was suppressed for several months below detection on the sensitive estradiol test. Sort of like going into menopause, I observed skin changes among other things...not fun. YOu miss your estradiol if you don't have enough of it.

# Thread: Boston Harbor Dip
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### N~Doro — 2012-05-03T18:19:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21011>

Water temp: 48-50, air temp 53 and very windy off the water.

So, last weekend I had a birthday. Other than being treated like the bees knees for two days, I mentioned to DH that I was hoping to take a little dip in Boston Harbor at Castle Island a couple miles from where we live. He was amused but didnâ€™t say much as we headed off for a lovely hike in the wayburbs. Accidently happened upon a climbing rope and two sets of rings in the woods where someone has set up a parkour course on one of the trails. The higher set was just out of my reach, so with just a wee leap I was doing my first unassisted pull-up in my 51 years (been working on this for months with a stretchy band). Almost got three good ones. Way cool.

Drove back to Boston just as the sun was going down and without a word DH drove to the beach. Thought he was calling my bluff. I wasnâ€™t really prepared so had to go in the water in my t-shirt & skivvies instead of a bathing suit. I walked right in up to my waist. At that point it started to drop off faster though and I freaked a bit since it was now dark out. Stinging legs, shivery from the wind but managed to stay in for a few minutes. Probably less than ten, but DH kept yelling out to me â€œI thought you were just going to walk in and back out â€“ how much longer - Iâ€™m freezingâ€￾. He did manage to take a blurry picture as he stood on shore. It was pretty great to be in the ocean at night with downtown Boston as the backdrop. Not a bad birthday all around.

Canâ€™t wait to do it again.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/21086=176-castle_island4_29.jpg)

### MJ* — 2012-05-03T18:52:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21026>

> **N~Doro;21086 wrote:** Water temp: 48-50, air temp 53 and very windy off the water.
>
>
>
> So, last weekend I had a birthday. Other than being treated like the bees knees for two days, I mentioned to DH that I was hoping to take a little dip in Boston Harbor at Castle Island a couple miles from where we live. He was amused but didn’t say much as we headed off for a lovely hike in the wayburbs. Accidently happened upon a climbing rope and two sets of rings in the woods where someone has set up a parkour course on one of the trails. The higher set was just out of my reach, so with just a wee leap I was doing my first unassisted pull-up in my 51 years (been working on this for months with a stretchy band). Almost got three good ones. Way cool.
>
>
>
> Drove back to Boston just as the sun was going down and without a word DH drove to the beach. Thought he was calling my bluff. I wasn’t really prepared so had to go in the water in my t-shirt & skivvies instead of a bathing suit. I walked right in up to my waist. At that point it started to drop off faster though and I freaked a bit since it was now dark out. Stinging legs, shivery from the wind but managed to stay in for a few minutes. Probably less than ten, but DH kept yelling out to me “I thought you were just going to walk in and back out – how much longer - I’m freezing”. He did manage to take a blurry picture as he stood on shore. It was pretty great to be in the ocean at night with downtown Boston as the backdrop. Not a bad birthday all around.
>
> Can’t wait to do it again.
>
>
>
>
> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/21086=176-castle_island4_29.jpg)

Happy Birthday!!! And Awesome job!!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-03T18:56:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21029>

Good for you!! Happy birthday! I see many more pulls in your future....

### indigogirl — 2012-05-03T19:01:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21034>

What a great way to celebrate your birthday!

### N~Doro — 2012-05-03T20:10:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21064>

> **Patty Cakes;21104 wrote:** Good for you!! Happy birthday! I see many more pulls in your future....

Thanks everyone. Hopefully some better pictures in my future as well. Next time, daylight so you can actually see the harbor.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-03T22:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21098>

The higher set was just out of my reach, so with just a wee leap I was doing my first unassisted pull-up in my 51 years (been working on this for months with a stretchy band). Almost got three good ones. Way cool.
>

> Drove back to Boston just as the sun was going down and without a word DH drove to the beach. Thought he was calling my bluff. I wasn’t really prepared so had to go in the water in my t-shirt & skivvies instead of a bathing suit. I walked right in up to my waist. At that point it started to drop off faster though and I freaked a bit since it was now dark out. Stinging legs, shivery from the wind but managed to stay in for a few minutes. Probably less than ten, but DH kept yelling out to me “I thought you were just going to walk in and back out – how much longer - I’m freezing”. He did manage to take a blurry picture as he stood on shore. It was pretty great to be in the ocean at night with downtown Boston as the backdrop. Not a bad birthday all around.
>

>
>

> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/21086=176-castle_island4_29.jpg)

I like that you did it at night!!!! Why did not you go in all the way?

### N~Doro — 2012-05-03T22:48:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boston-harbor-dip.1264/post-21110>

> **Jack Kruse;21173 wrote:** I like that you did it at night!!!! Why did not you go in all the way?

Meant to of course, but I actually haven't been swimming there even during the day. It started to drop off quickly and the shore was looking pretty far off as I was a shiverin' away - all the while knowing that DH could barely see me and would _not_ be coming in after me. Just got spooked. The water was about 6 degrees colder than I have attempted with a bath, but probably would have been easier if i had gone up to my neck because of the wind. It was pretty cool to walk onto a beach in Boston and drop trau without anyone around.

Anyway, enough excuses. Next time should be a breeze.

Going to CT this weekend and DH promised to take me to a childhood fishing/swimming hole.
